proposals.title: Development of the Protocol Review and Evaluation Checklist (PREC): A consensus-based tool for ethics committees and funders
proposals.motivation: While reporting guidelines like SPIRIT and CONSORT have improved the completeness of study protocols and publications, there is no standardized tool for the bodies that review and approve them. Ethics committees and funders currently assess protocols using ad-hoc or institution-specific criteria, leading to inconsistent evaluations, potential oversight of critical methodological or ethical flaws, and inefficiency for researchers navigating multiple reviews. A standardized checklist would enhance the rigor, transparency, and fairness of the protocol review process itself.
proposals.hypothesis: A consensus-based, structured checklist for protocol review will improve the consistency and comprehensiveness of evaluations by ethics committees and funders, as measured by inter-rater reliability and coverage of key methodological and ethical domains compared to existing ad-hoc practices.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, consensus-building approach. First, a scoping review of literature (e.g., funder guidelines, ethics committee terms of reference) and analysis of existing reporting guidelines (SPIRIT, CONSORT) will generate a preliminary item pool. Second, a multi-round, online modified Delphi process will be conducted with a purposively sampled international panel of experts, including ethicists, funder representatives, methodologies, and patient partners. Third, the refined checklist will be pilot-tested for usability and reliability by having multiple reviewers from different ethics committees and funding panels apply it to a sample of real, anonymized protocols.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first rigorously developed, consensus-based tool specifically designed for the systematic review of research protocols by gatekeepers. It will contribute to the science of research on research (meta-research) by providing a methodological standard for protocol evaluation, potentially reducing review burden and improving study quality from the outset.
proposals.novelty: This work shifts the focus from reporting guidelines for researchers to an evaluation tool for reviewers, addressing the 'dual problem' of incomplete reporting and inconsistent review. It synthesizes Moher's expertise in guideline development with the emerging pre-2026 emphasis on improving research quality at the protocol stage through tools like SPIRIT.

proposals.title: Enhancing the Usability and Impact of Reporting Guidelines: A User-Centered Design and Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT, PRISMA) are critical for research transparency and reproducibility. However, their impact is limited by poor usability; they are often presented as static checklists that are difficult for authors to apply correctly and for readers to navigate efficiently. Applying user experience (UX) design principles to their structure and presentation can directly address this gap, potentially increasing guideline adherence and improving the clarity of published research reports.
proposals.hypothesis: Reporting guidelines redesigned using formal UX principles will lead to significantly higher rates of correct item identification and comprehension among both research authors and systematic reviewers, compared to the standard checklist format.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study. First, a user-centered design phase will involve iterative prototyping with key stakeholders (authors, reviewers, editors) to redesign a core guideline (e.g., CONSORT for Abstracts). This will employ established UX methods like persona development, task analysis, and usability testing. Second, we will conduct a randomized controlled experiment, where participants will be assigned to use either the standard checklist or the UX-optimized format to extract or report specific trial information. The primary outcome will be the accuracy and speed of information retrieval/completion, analyzed using chi-square tests and t-tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence on how UX design can improve the practical utility of reporting guidelines. It will yield a tested, user-optimized template for a major guideline and a methodological framework for applying UX design to other research quality tools, moving beyond their traditional document-centric format.
proposals.novelty: While guideline development often involves consensus, the systematic application of formal UX design methodologies—treating the guideline as an interface between research producers and consumers—is a novel and untested approach to enhancing research reporting and critical appraisal.

proposals.title: Developing a Prototype for Adaptive Reporting Guidelines in Systematic Reviews: A Delphi Consensus and Feasibility Study
proposals.motivation: Current reporting guidelines, like PRISMA, are static checklists that may not be optimally efficient or relevant for all review types (e.g., rapid reviews, scoping reviews, reviews of complex interventions). This one-size-fits-all approach can lead to unnecessary reporting burdens or omissions, hindering the transparency and utility of evidence syntheses. An adaptive framework that tailors reporting requirements to the review's specific methodology and data characteristics could improve both compliance and the quality of reported information.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, logic-based framework for reporting guidelines, which adapts its recommended items based on user-inputted review characteristics, will be judged by experts as more feasible and useful than a static checklist for ensuring transparent reporting across diverse systematic review types.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study. First, a modified Delphi exercise with methodologies, journal editors, and end-users will identify core and conditional reporting items for major review types (systematic reviews, rapid reviews, scoping reviews). Second, we will develop a prototype web-based tool using a rule-based algorithm (e.g., a decision tree or if-then logic engine) that presents a tailored checklist based on user selections for review type, data synthesis methods, and risk of bias assessment tools. Third, we will conduct a feasibility test where systematic review teams use the prototype and provide feedback on usability via validated surveys (e.g., System Usability Scale) and qualitative interviews.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a functional prototype of the first adaptive reporting guideline system, moving the field beyond static documents. It will provide empirical data on expert consensus for conditional reporting items and initial evidence on the feasibility and perceived utility of a dynamic approach, directly informing future iterations of major guidelines like PRISMA.
proposals.novelty: While extensions of PRISMA exist for specific review types, the concept of a single, interactive guideline that dynamically adjusts its content based on a logic algorithm is novel. It challenges the foundational paradigm of fixed checklists in research reporting.

proposals.title: Developing and Piloting a Pre-Submission Verification Checklist for Systematic Reviews: An Adaptation of High-Reliability Industry Practices
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of reporting guidelines like PRISMA, systematic reviews (SRs) frequently contain critical errors and omissions that compromise their reliability and utility. The current peer-review process is often insufficient to catch these issues post-submission, leading to resource waste and potential misinformation. High-reliability industries like aviation use standardized, mandatory pre-flight checklists to prevent human error, a concept not yet formally applied to the SR submission workflow.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mandatory, structured pre-submission verification checklist, adapted from high-reliability industry principles, will significantly reduce the frequency of critical reporting and methodological omissions in submitted systematic review manuscripts compared to the current standard practice of relying on author discretion and post-hoc peer review.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study. First, a modified Delphi process with SR methodologies, journal editors, and guideline developers will adapt aviation checklist principles (e.g., challenge-response, read-do, temporal ordering) into a draft SR verification checklist. Second, we will conduct a randomized controlled trial. Manuscripts from authors preparing to submit an SR to a participating journal will be randomized to use the checklist or proceed with usual practice. The primary outcome will be the proportion of manuscripts with critical omissions (against PRISMA 2020 items) identified by independent assessors blinded to group allocation. We will use chi-square tests to compare groups and logistic regression to adjust for potential confounders.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first empirically developed verification tool designed to prevent errors *before* SR submission, directly addressing gaps in the research workflow. It will provide evidence on whether a procedural intervention can improve the completeness of submitted SRs, potentially reducing peer-review burden and post-publication corrections. The findings will inform journal policy and author guidance, moving beyond passive dissemination of guidelines to active implementation support.
proposals.novelty: While checklists exist for reporting (PRISMA) and critical appraisal, applying the formal, procedural 'pre-flight' checklist methodology from high-reliability industries to the scholarly submission process is a novel translational concept. It shifts the focus from post-hoc assessment to proactive error prevention at the point of manuscript finalization.

proposals.title: Development and validation of an automated tool for assessing reporting completeness in systematic reviews: the Auto-RISK (Reporting Integrity Scoring Kit) project
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread adoption of reporting guidelines like PRISMA, the completeness of reporting in published systematic reviews remains highly variable, undermining their reliability and utility. Manual assessment of reporting quality is resource-intensive and subjective, creating a barrier to large-scale evaluation and meta-research. An automated, scalable tool is needed to efficiently audit reporting practices and identify common deficiencies across the literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a natural language processing (NLP) tool, trained on a corpus of systematic reviews with expert-annotated PRISMA checklists, can reliably and accurately score the reporting completeness of new reviews, achieving high agreement with human assessors.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised machine learning model using a BERT-based architecture (specifically BioBERT or SciBERT, pre-trained on biomedical literature) fine-tuned for multi-label classification. The training dataset will be created by manually annotating a random sample of 500 systematic reviews from PubMed for adherence to the 27-item PRISMA 2020 checklist. The model will be trained to identify the presence or absence of each PRISMA item in the full text of a review. Performance will be validated on a held-out test set, with agreement measured against independent human raters using Cohen's kappa and F1 scores.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first open-source, validated automated tool for assessing PRISMA adherence, enabling large-scale audits of reporting quality. It will provide empirical data on the most frequently under-reported items and can be integrated into editorial and peer-review workflows to prompt authors for missing information, thereby potentially improving reporting standards.
proposals.novelty: While previous tools automate search for reporting guidelines statements, this tool aims to assess the substantive completeness of reporting for each guideline item using deep learning, moving beyond simple keyword detection to semantic understanding of methodological reporting.

proposals.title: Assessing the Efficacy of Checklist Formats for Conveying Complex Methodological Standards: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Reporting guidelines like CONSORT are widely disseminated as checklists to improve research transparency and reproducibility. However, their effectiveness hinges on authors' comprehension and correct application. Pre-2026 evidence suggests poor adherence persists, raising the question of whether the checklist format itself is a suboptimal vehicle for conveying complex methodological concepts, especially to authors with diverse expertise and primary languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a checklist format supplemented with integrated, context-specific instructional modules will lead to superior comprehension and intended application of methodological standards compared to a standalone checklist among authors preparing manuscripts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group randomized controlled trial. Participants (corresponding authors of recently submitted biomedical manuscripts) will be randomized to receive either: 1) the standard CONSORT for Abstracts checklist, or 2) an enhanced intervention integrating the checklist with interactive, just-in-time explanations and examples for each item. The primary outcome will be the score on a validated knowledge transfer test assessing comprehension and application. Secondary outcomes include usability ratings and the completeness of reporting in a mock manuscript exercise. We will use an intention-to-treat analysis with appropriate statistical tests (e.g., t-tests, regression) to compare groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the optimal design of reporting guideline tools, moving beyond their mere dissemination. It will contribute directly to the science of research reporting by testing a theory-informed intervention aimed at improving knowledge translation from guideline developers to end-users.
proposals.novelty: While checklists are ubiquitous, their format is rarely questioned or experimentally tested as a knowledge translation strategy. This study applies a rigorous RCT design—a method central to Moher's work—to evaluate the checklist format itself, a foundational but untested assumption in the reporting guideline ecosystem.

proposals.title: Developing and Evaluating a Dynamic, Multi-Layered Reporting Framework for Clinical Trial Results: A Delphi Consensus Study and Feasibility Pilot
proposals.motivation: The current standard of reporting clinical trial results in a single, static manuscript is increasingly recognized as inadequate. It forces a linear narrative, often obscures methodological detail and negative findings, and fails to serve the diverse needs of different user groups (e.g., clinicians, systematic reviewers, patients). This limitation, highlighted by ongoing discussions about the reproducibility crisis and the need for better data sharing, hinders the efficient translation of research into practice and evidence synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, dynamic reporting framework that presents trial results in multiple, interlinked layers (e.g., a core executive summary, detailed methods, interactive results, and raw data) will be deemed more usable, transparent, and valuable for key stakeholder groups than the traditional single-document report.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-phase study. First, a scoping review will map existing concepts of layered or modular reporting. Second, a modified Delphi study with a diverse panel (trialists, journal editors, systematic reviewers, patient partners) will iteratively develop consensus on the core components and structure of a dynamic reporting framework. Third, we will conduct a feasibility pilot, applying the draft framework to a sample of completed trials (e.g., from ClinicalTrials.gov) and testing its usability with systematic reviewers using task-based scenarios and validated usability tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a concrete, stakeholder-informed prototype for a next-generation trial reporting format. It will provide empirical evidence on the perceived value and practical challenges of moving beyond the static manuscript, directly informing future reporting guidelines (like CONSORT extensions) and journal publishing policies.
proposals.novelty: While data sharing and reporting checklists are established, this proposal moves upstream to fundamentally re-engineer the *format* of the primary report itself, integrating stakeholder consensus with a practical pilot test to bridge conceptual design and real-world application.

proposals.title: Automated Auditing of Randomized Trial Reports: A Natural Language Processing Tool for Assessing Adherence to the CONSORT Statement
proposals.motivation: Incomplete reporting of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) remains a persistent problem, undermining the reliability of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Manual assessment of CONSORT adherence is time-consuming and resource-intensive, limiting the scale and frequency of reporting audits. An automated, scalable method is needed to efficiently monitor reporting quality across the vast corpus of published trial literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised natural language processing (NLP) model, trained on manually annotated RCT reports, can accurately and reliably classify the presence or absence of key CONSORT checklist items in trial publications.
proposals.method: We will develop a corpus of RCT reports from PubMed, manually annotated for a subset of critical CONSORT items (e.g., randomization, blinding, participant flow). Using this gold-standard dataset, we will train and validate supervised machine learning models. Initial models will include a Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) architecture, fine-tuned for sequence classification, and a traditional NLP pipeline using support vector machines with features derived from sentence embeddings and rule-based patterns. Model performance will be assessed using precision, recall, and F1-score against the manual annotations.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a proof-of-concept, open-source NLP tool for the automated assessment of CONSORT adherence, enabling large-scale audits of reporting quality. It will provide empirical data on the feasibility and accuracy of computational methods for guideline adherence checking, a novel application in research on research.
proposals.novelty: While NLP has been used for tasks like citation screening, its application to the granular, checklist-based auditing of reporting guideline adherence in trial reports is novel. This moves beyond simple keyword searches to a context-aware classification of whether specific methodological details are adequately reported.

proposals.title: Inferring Risk of Bias from Reporting Patterns: A Machine Learning Approach to Detect Omissions in Clinical Trial Publications
proposals.motivation: Poor reporting in clinical trials obscures methodological flaws and biases, undermining evidence synthesis and clinical decision-making. Existing tools like the Cochrane Risk of Bias (RoB) 2.0 require manual assessment, which is resource-intensive and subjective. An automated method to flag potential bias from reporting omissions would expedite systematic reviews and improve the reliability of meta-analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: Patterns of omission and linguistic hedging in the reporting of trial methods are predictive of specific domains of methodological bias, such as selection, performance, and detection bias, as defined by established risk-of-bias frameworks.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised machine learning pipeline. A labeled dataset will be created by extracting text from a large corpus of published trial reports (e.g., from PubMed Central) and pairing each with its corresponding, manually-assessed RoB judgment from systematic reviews (e.g., Cochrane reviews). Using natural language processing (NLP), we will engineer features capturing omissions against CONSORT checklist items, linguistic uncertainty, and section-specific term frequencies. We will train and compare the performance of classifiers, including logistic regression, random forests, and a bidirectional LSTM neural network, to predict RoB domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source algorithm to screen trial reports for high risk of bias, serving as a rapid triage tool for systematic reviewers and journal editors. It will also provide empirical evidence linking specific reporting patterns to methodological weaknesses, informing future reporting guideline updates.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on adherence checklists, this approach frames incomplete reporting as an inverse problem to directly infer underlying methodological bias, not just reporting quality. It leverages pre-2026 NLP architectures on a novel dataset linking full-text reports to authoritative RoB judgments.

proposals.title: A semi-automated tool for data extraction and harmonization in network meta-analysis: A development and validation study to relax the assumption of consistent reporting formats
proposals.motivation: Network meta-analysis (NMA) relies on consistent data extraction from diverse studies, but reporting heterogeneity is a major bottleneck, increasing review time and error risk. Existing tools like RobotReviewer and ExaCT require structured formats, failing to adapt to the highly variable reporting of outcomes, populations, and interventions in real-world publications. This inefficiency contradicts the push for rapid, reproducible evidence synthesis and data sharing championed by initiatives like CONSORT and PRISMA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid natural language processing (NLP) tool, combining rule-based parsing with a pre-trained transformer model fine-tuned on biomedical text, can accurately extract and harmonize key NMA parameters (e.g., arm-level means, counts, standard errors) from PDFs with heterogeneous reporting structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular pipeline. First, a rule-based module will identify common reporting patterns (e.g., 'mean (SD)'). Second, we will fine-tune a BERT-based model (BioBERT or SciBERT, pre-2026 state-of-the-art) on a manually annotated corpus of randomized trial PDFs to extract entities and relationships from less structured text. The tool will output structured data mapped to a harmonized schema (e.g., for continuous outcomes). We will validate its performance against a gold-standard, manually extracted dataset from a published NMA, measuring precision, recall, and time savings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source tool to reduce the manual burden of data extraction for NMA, directly supporting systematic reviewers. It will advance methodological research on automating evidence synthesis and operationalize the principles of data sharing by making analytic inputs more readily reusable. The tool will be disseminated with guidelines for its use, aligning with journal and reporting guideline initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While NLP tools exist for data extraction, this proposal uniquely focuses on the specific challenge of harmonizing *for NMA* from highly heterogeneous inputs, moving beyond simple extraction to structured data integration. It combines Moher's expertise in reporting guidelines and replication with state-of-the-art (pre-2026) NLP, applying it to a concrete synthesis bottleneck.

proposals.title: Developing an Adaptive Reporting Tool for Network Meta-Analysis (ART-NMA): A proof-of-concept study to improve adherence to reporting guidelines through dynamic question generation
proposals.motivation: Current reporting guidelines, like PRISMA-NMA, are static checklists that may not be optimally efficient or effective for complex, heterogeneous study designs like network meta-analysis (NMA). This one-size-fits-all approach can lead to author burden, incomplete reporting, and reduced reproducibility. An interactive system that adapts to the specific NMA design could improve the completeness and accuracy of reporting, directly addressing gaps identified in surveys of systematic reviewers.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive, logic-based reporting system that tailors questions to the specific characteristics of an NMA (e.g., network geometry, outcome type, model choice) will produce more complete and relevant reporting item responses compared to a static PRISMA-NMA checklist, as assessed by expert reviewers.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype web-based tool using a rule-based engine (e.g., a decision tree or finite-state machine) programmed with logic derived from the PRISMA-NMA 2015 statement and its explanations. The system will first ask key design questions (e.g., 'Is your network connected?', 'Are you using a Bayesian or frequentist model?'). Subsequent questions and prompts will be dynamically generated based on prior answers, hiding irrelevant items and providing context-specific guidance. We will test the tool's performance by comparing the reporting completeness of mock NMA protocols generated using the adaptive tool versus the static checklist.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional prototype demonstrating the feasibility of adaptive reporting systems, moving beyond static documents. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for implementing reporting guidelines as interactive software, potentially increasing guideline utility and adherence. The findings will inform future iterations of reporting standards and digital scholarly infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While CONSORT and PRISMA extensions exist, they remain static PDFs. This project operationalizes reporting guidance as an interactive, logic-driven system tailored to NMA's unique complexities, a significant shift from the current checklist paradigm. It integrates Moher's expertise in guidelines with modern digital implementation strategies.

proposals.title: Developing the PRISMA-NMA-MA (Machine-Actionable) Extension: Reporting Guidelines for Computationally Executable Network Meta-Analysis
proposals.motivation: The automation of evidence synthesis is accelerating, yet the reporting of network meta-analyses (NMAs) remains tailored for human readers, creating a bottleneck for computational systems. Existing PRISMA-NMA guidelines do not specify the structured data and code required for automated systems to reliably ingest, validate, and potentially re-run or update analyses. This limits the scalability, reproducibility, and rapid integration of NMA results into living systematic reviews and guideline development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a formal extension to the PRISMA-NMA statement, specifying mandatory reporting elements for computational actionability, will significantly improve the machine-readability, reproducibility, and reusability of published NMA results.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage consensus project. First, a scoping review will identify existing efforts in machine-actionable reporting and computational needs for NMA. Second, a Delphi survey involving methodologies, systematic reviewers, journal editors, and informaticians will iteratively rate candidate reporting items. Third, a consensus meeting will finalize the checklist and flow diagram extensions. The technical specification will mandate the provision of structured data (e.g., adjacency matrices, treatment codes, effect estimates with full variance-covariance matrices in a standard format like JSON) and executable analytic code (e.g., R/Python/Stan scripts) alongside the manuscript.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a formally endorsed, stakeholder-vetted reporting guideline (PRISMA-NMA-MA) to bridge the gap between human-readable and machine-actionable NMA reporting. It will directly enable the development of automated tools for evidence synthesis by providing a clear standard for data and code sharing, thereby enhancing the reliability and efficiency of secondary research.
proposals.novelty: While PRISMA extensions exist, none are explicitly designed from the ground up to serve computational agents as primary 'readers,' moving beyond data sharing recommendations to define a minimum reporting standard for computational executability in complex multivariate meta-analyses.

proposals.title: A Knowledge Graph Framework for the Formalization and Querying of Treatment Networks in Systematic Reviews
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews and network meta-analyses (NMAs) are central to evidence-based medicine, but their underlying treatment networks—the structure of which interventions have been compared directly and indirectly—are often presented as static figures or tables. This limits the ability to query, share, and computationally reason about the evidence base. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need, reflected in CONSORT and PRISMA extensions for NMAs, to improve the clarity, reproducibility, and accessibility of complex evidence structures.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing treatment networks and their associated evidence using Semantic Web standards (RDF, OWL) will enable more precise querying, automated consistency checks, and facilitate the sharing and reuse of network meta-analysis data structures.
proposals.method: We will develop a formal ontology for treatment networks, extending existing biomedical ontologies. Using data from published NMAs (e.g., from Cochrane), we will implement a pipeline to convert network data (treatments, comparisons, study identifiers) into a knowledge graph. Specific technical steps will involve using the RDF data model, the Web Ontology Language (OWL) for axiomatization, and SPARQL for querying. We will test the framework's utility by replicating key queries from sample reviews and assessing its ability to identify evidence gaps and inconsistencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a reusable, open-source framework for representing treatment networks as FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data. It will contribute a concrete methodological advance in evidence synthesis reporting, supporting the goals of data sharing and research transparency advocated by initiatives like the CONSORT and PRISMA-NMA statements.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge graphs are used in biomedicine for data integration, their application to formally encode the *structure* of evidence from systematic reviews and NMAs for enhanced querying and reasoning is novel. This moves beyond static reporting guidelines to an executable, semantic representation of the evidence network.

proposals.title: Developing a Minimal Reporting Checklist for Rapid and Scoping Network Meta-Analyses: A CONSORT and PRISMA-NMA Extension Project
proposals.motivation: Network meta-analyses (NMAs) are resource-intensive. There is a growing need for rapid or scoping NMAs to inform preliminary decisions, such as prioritizing topics for full systematic reviews or urgent health policy. However, existing comprehensive guidelines like PRISMA-NMA are not tailored to these abbreviated reviews, risking inconsistent, incomplete, or misleading reporting that could compromise their utility for decision-makers.
proposals.hypothesis: A consensus-based, minimal reporting checklist specifically for rapid and scoping NMAs will improve the transparency, reproducibility, and interpretability of these reviews, thereby increasing their reliability for preliminary decision-making.
proposals.method: The project will follow established guideline development methodology, including: (1) a systematic scoping review of rapid/scoping NMA methods and reporting practices; (2) a multi-round, online Delphi survey involving key stakeholders (systematic reviewers, methodologies, journal editors, and end-users) to generate and refine checklist items; (3) a consensus meeting (virtual or in-person) to finalize the checklist; and (4) the development of an Explanation and Elaboration document. The process will be modeled on the development of PRISMA and CONSORT extensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formal, consensus-based reporting guideline for rapid and scoping NMAs. It will provide a practical tool for authors, peer reviewers, and journals, and establish a standard for evaluating the credibility of these increasingly common but currently under-standardized reviews.
proposals.novelty: While PRISMA-NMA exists for full NMAs and PRISMA-ScR for scoping reviews, no guideline bridges these for the specific context of a rapid, decision-focused NMA. This project explicitly addresses the trade-off between comprehensiveness and speed, defining the minimal reporting elements necessary for credibility.

proposals.title: Development of a Critical Appraisal Tool for Network Meta-Analysis Reporting: The NMA-CAT Guideline
proposals.motivation: Network meta-analysis (NMA) is a complex and influential method for comparing multiple interventions, but its reporting quality is variable. Existing tools like PRISMA-NMA focus on reporting completeness for authors, not on critical appraisal for readers, reviewers, and guideline developers. A dedicated, user-friendly tool is urgently needed to help these stakeholders systematically evaluate the methodological rigor and credibility of published NMAs, which directly impacts clinical and policy decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, consensus-based critical appraisal tool, developed by integrating existing reporting standards and methodological guidance, will provide a reliable and valid method for assessing the quality of reported NMAs, leading to more consistent and transparent evaluations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage guideline development project. First, a scoping review will map existing NMA reporting items, methodological checklists (e.g., from Cochrane, ISPOR), and bias assessment frameworks. Second, a multi-round Delphi survey involving methodologies, systematic reviewers, journal editors, and statisticians will be used to achieve consensus on core appraisal items. Third, the tool will be piloted and refined through user testing with peer reviewers and readers, assessing its usability and inter-rater reliability.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formal critical appraisal tool (NMA-CAT) specifically designed for readers and peer reviewers of NMAs, complementing the author-focused PRISMA-NMA. It will enhance the critical consumption of NMA evidence, improve peer review quality, and ultimately support more reliable evidence synthesis in healthcare decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While PRISMA-NMA tells authors what to report, this tool provides a complementary framework for judging the reported content, filling a critical gap in the evidence ecosystem. It operationalizes complex NMA methodology into a practical, consensus-driven checklist for end-users, which is a novel application of guideline development principles in this domain.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating an Adaptive Protocol Synthesis and Reporting (APSR) System for Clinical Trials: A Feasibility Study
proposals.motivation: Public trial registries (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov) often contain sparse, unstructured protocol information, hindering reproducibility and systematic review. Concurrently, the static nature of reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT) fails to adapt to a study's specific complexity, stage, or stakeholder needs, leading to reporting burdens or omissions. An integrated system that synthesizes a structured protocol from sparse data and then tailors reporting dynamically could address both inefficiencies, enhancing transparency and reducing research waste.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning system can accurately infer a structured study protocol from sparse public registry entries, and this synthesized protocol can be used to dynamically generate tailored, context-aware reporting recommendations that improve reporting completeness and efficiency compared to static guidelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system. First, a natural language processing (NLP) model (e.g., a fine-tuned BERT variant) will be trained on paired sparse registry entries and full-text protocols to infer a detailed, structured protocol. Second, a rule-based and machine learning classifier will monitor protocol attributes (e.g., risk, design) and review stage to dynamically select from a library of reporting modules derived from CONSORT, SPIRIT, and other guidelines. The system's output will be validated via expert consensus (Delphi method) on a sample of synthesized protocols and their adaptive reporting outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a novel, integrated system that links protocol inference with adaptive reporting. It will contribute a validated framework for reducing redundancy in protocol reporting and a methodology for dynamic, stakeholder-specific guideline application, directly addressing calls for more efficient and transparent research practices.
proposals.novelty: While protocol templating and reporting checklists exist separately, this system uniquely creates a feedback loop between them, using machine learning to synthesize a protocol from sparse public data and then using that structure to drive context-aware, adaptive reporting recommendations—a non-obvious fusion of informatics and guideline science.

proposals.title: The VERIFY-SR Project: Development and Feasibility Testing of a Digital Workflow System for Verifiable, Evidence-Based Systematic Review Conduct
proposals.motivation: Despite established reporting guidelines like PRISMA, systematic reviews often suffer from poor reproducibility and verification challenges. The credibility of evidence synthesis is undermined by the inability to externally audit whether the reported methods were actually followed, a problem highlighted by replication studies and surveys on data sharing. A shift from passive guidance to active, documented implementation is needed to strengthen trust in synthesized evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured digital workflow system that mandates the creation of timestamped, versioned process artifacts during review conduct will significantly increase the verifiability and auditability of systematic reviews compared to standard practice using guidelines alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based platform integrating established tools (e.g., for screening, data extraction) with a novel process-logging architecture. Key conduct steps will trigger mandatory documentation via structured forms and automated capture of user actions, stored with cryptographic hashes in an immutable ledger (using a simplified blockchain-inspired timestamping service). We will employ a mixed-methods feasibility study, where review teams use the prototype to conduct a review, followed by quantitative analysis of artifact completeness and qualitative interviews on usability and perceived burden.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a functional prototype of a verifiable workflow system and initial evidence on its feasibility. It will provide a concrete model for linking review process to output transparently, advancing the field beyond static guidelines towards dynamic, evidence-based conduct. The findings will inform future standards for review auditability and data sharing.
proposals.novelty: While checklists and registries exist, this system actively enforces and documents methodological decisions in real-time, creating an immutable audit trail. It moves from recommending transparency to engineering it directly into the review conduct environment, a proactive approach distinct from post-hoc reporting or data sharing initiatives.

proposals.title: Developing a Process Guideline for the Conduct of Systematic Reviews (PRISMA-C): A Delphi Consensus Study
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines like PRISMA focus on reporting, not the conduct of systematic reviews. This leaves critical upstream decisions about search strategies, data extraction, and risk-of-bias assessment without standardized, evidence-based guidance, potentially compromising review validity and reproducibility. A conduct guideline is essential to improve methodological rigor and efficiency, especially for rapid reviews and complex topics like complementary medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: A formal consensus process involving methodologies, statisticians, and experienced reviewers will yield a core set of actionable, evidence-informed recommendations for the conduct of systematic reviews that are distinct from reporting standards.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-round modified Delphi study. First, a steering committee will draft an initial list of candidate items based on a scoping review of existing methodological guidance and known conduct challenges (e.g., from replication studies). Second, an international panel of ~30 experts will rate each item for importance on a 9-point Likert scale over multiple rounds, with controlled feedback. Third, a consensus meeting will finalize the guideline (PRISMA-C) and develop an explanatory document.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formal, consensus-based guideline specifically for the *process* of conducting systematic reviews. It will provide a practical framework to improve methodological decisions, enhance the reliability of review outputs, and complement existing reporting standards like PRISMA and CONSORT.
proposals.novelty: While PRISMA addresses *what* to report, this project innovatively focuses on *how* to perform key review steps, shifting the focus upstream from reporting to conduct. It synthesizes scattered methodological advice into a single, authoritative standard.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating a Version-Controlled, AI-Augmented Single-Reviewer Workflow for Systematic Reviews: A Feasibility and Reliability Study
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews are resource-intensive, and single-reviewer screening with verification is a proposed rapid review method, but it lacks transparency and risks bias. Current AI tools for screening act as black boxes, and there is no standardized way to track the human-AI interaction or audit decision-making over time. This limits reproducibility and trust in accelerated review methods.
proposals.hypothesis: A version-controlled workflow that logs all human and AI decisions in a structured, time-stamped audit trail will produce a systematic review with comparable reliability to dual-reviewer methods while providing superior transparency, enabling the detection of decision drift and the testing of conclusion robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype web platform integrating a Git-based version control system (e.g., via the libgit2 library) with an AI screening assistant based on a pre-2026 transformer model (e.g., BERT or BioBERT) fine-tuned on existing review corpora. The platform will log every AI suggestion and human action as a commit. We will conduct a case study comparing the workflow's output on a sample of published reviews against their original dual-reviewer results, measuring agreement (Cohen's kappa) and analyzing the commit history for patterns of uncertainty and override.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept framework for transparent, auditable, and efficient single-reviewer systematic reviews. It will yield concrete guidelines for implementing version control in evidence synthesis and produce a novel dataset of human-AI decision interactions for future research on reviewer consistency and machine learning reliability.
proposals.novelty: While AI-assisted screening and the CONSORT-style reporting of review methods exist, the application of granular version control to create a replayable, living audit trail of the human-AI collaborative process is novel. This moves beyond static reporting to dynamic, executable research artifacts.

proposals.title: Developing a Rigorous Protocol for the Translation and Cultural Adaptation of the PRISMA Reporting Guideline: An Adaptation of the CONSORT Model
proposals.motivation: The PRISMA statement is a cornerstone for transparent systematic review reporting, but its impact is limited by language and cultural barriers. Many researchers and peer reviewers in non-English speaking settings lack access to a formally adapted, conceptually equivalent version, hindering global adherence and the quality of evidence synthesis. Addressing this gap is critical for improving the conduct and reporting of systematic reviews worldwide.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the structured, multi-step translation and cultural adaptation model developed for the CONSORT statement to the PRISMA guideline will yield linguistically accurate, culturally relevant, and conceptually equivalent versions that are more usable and effective in diverse research settings than simple translation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the established CONSORT translation model, which involves forward translation by two independent translators, synthesis, back-translation, review by an expert committee (including methodologies, linguists, and end-users), and pre-testing with systematic reviewers in the target setting. This process will be piloted for two non-English languages. We will employ cognitive interviews and usability tests during the pre-testing phase to assess clarity, comprehensibility, and cultural relevance of the adapted PRISMA checklists and flow diagrams.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formally validated protocol for translating and culturally adapting PRISMA, providing a reproducible roadmap for future adaptations. It will directly contribute to global equity in evidence synthesis by creating high-quality, accessible guideline versions, ultimately aiming to improve the reporting quality of non-English language systematic reviews.
proposals.novelty: While translation models exist for clinical trial guidelines like CONSORT, this represents a novel, formal application of this rigorous methodology to the field of systematic review reporting guidelines. The work moves beyond informal translation to create a standardized adaptation protocol specifically for PRISMA, ensuring conceptual equivalence is maintained.

proposals.title: Developing and Piloting a Provenance-Tracking Framework for Transparent and Reproducible Systematic Reviews: A Feasibility Study
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews are foundational to evidence-based medicine, yet their reproducibility is hampered by incomplete reporting of the many subjective decisions made during the review process. Current guidelines (e.g., PRISMA) improve reporting of *what* was done but do not systematically capture the *why* behind critical decisions on study inclusion, data extraction, and risk-of-bias assessments, limiting auditability and the potential for replication or re-analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, machine-readable logging system for reviewer decisions and rationales during a systematic review is feasible and will produce a comprehensive provenance graph that enhances the transparency, auditability, and analytical potential of the review process.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype web-based platform extending the Open Science Framework. Reviewers will be prompted via structured forms to log decisions (e.g., excluding a study) using a controlled vocabulary from existing reporting guidelines (CONSORT, PRISMA). For each decision, they will tag the applicable rule, link to specific evidence (e.g., PDF snippets), and provide a free-text rationale. This data will be stored as linked JSON objects, forming a provenance graph. We will pilot the framework with experienced systematic reviewers conducting a rapid review, assessing feasibility via user surveys and analyzing the resulting graph's completeness and structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a functional prototype and preliminary evidence for a novel method of documenting systematic reviews. It will contribute directly to the fields of research methodology and reporting by providing a concrete tool to operationalize data sharing and analytic transparency, as called for in recent surveys, and will generate a novel dataset for studying decision-making patterns in evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While checklists and guidelines exist, this approach moves beyond documenting final choices to capturing the structured decision *process* itself in real-time, creating an auditable, machine-actionable provenance trail rather than a static narrative. It integrates version control for the rationale graph, not just documents.

proposals.title: Developing and Evaluating a Persona-Specific, Version-Controlled Interface for Transparent and Interactive Systematic Reviews
proposals.motivation: Current systematic review reporting, while guided by PRISMA and other guidelines, often presents a static, monolithic output. This limits utility for diverse end-users like clinicians, policymakers, and patients, who have different information needs and literacy levels. Furthermore, the detailed audit trail of decisions (e.g., study exclusion, data extraction changes) is typically buried in technical appendices, hindering true transparency, trust, and understanding of the evidence synthesis process.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating granular, Git-like version control of review decisions with user experience (UX) design principles tailored to specific end-user personas will produce a platform that significantly improves perceived transparency, trust, and usability of systematic reviews compared to standard PDF reports.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based platform using a three-layer architecture: a back-end database implementing a version-control model (akin to Git) to log every review action (commits, branches, diffs); a logic layer to map these technical logs to user-friendly explanations; and a front-end with distinct, user-tested interfaces for three primary personas (clinician, policymaker, patient). Development will follow an iterative, user-centered design process, informed by prior work on data sharing and reporting guidelines. The platform's efficacy will be evaluated via a mixed-methods study where representative users complete standardized tasks and provide feedback on transparency and usability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a functional prototype that operationalizes 'living' and 'explainable' evidence synthesis, moving transparency from an appendix to the core user experience. It will contribute new, validated methods for tailoring evidence dissemination and a framework for implementing rigorous version control in collaborative research synthesis, directly addressing calls for improved data and code sharing in reviews.
proposals.novelty: While version control is used in software development and open science, its application to create interactive, persona-specific explanations of a systematic review's decision-making history is novel. The synergy of rigorous audit trails with tailored UX design to enhance credibility and utility represents a significant advance beyond static reporting checklists.

proposals.title: A feasibility study and preliminary framework for applying distributed version control (Git) to enhance transparency and reproducibility in systematic review conduct
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews are complex, multi-stage projects where decisions about search strategies, study inclusion, and data extraction evolve, but current reporting (e.g., via PRISMA flow diagrams) provides only a static snapshot. This limits reproducibility and the ability to audit or understand the rationale behind changes. Pre-2026, there is growing emphasis on sharing review data and materials, yet no standardized method exists for tracking the detailed, iterative history of a review's development.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the core principles and infrastructure of distributed version control systems (specifically Git) to key stages of a systematic review will create a transparent, auditable, and machine-readable record of changes, thereby improving the reproducibility and integrity of the review process.
proposals.method: We will develop a conceptual framework mapping Git operations (commit, branch, merge, pull request) to systematic review steps (search strategy refinement, dual screening reconciliation, data extraction consensus). Using a sample of 3-5 ongoing reviews, we will implement a pilot using a standard Git repository (e.g., on GitHub) with structured file formats (e.g., CSV for inclusion decisions, plain text for search logs). We will employ Git's native diff and blame tools to track changes and conduct qualitative interviews with the review teams to assess feasibility, usability, and perceived value.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a proof-of-concept framework and a set of preliminary guidelines for using version control in evidence synthesis. It will directly inform future development of reporting guidelines (e.g., extensions to PRISMA) and specialized tools, moving the field toward a more open and reproducible workflow where the complete provenance of a review is accessible.
proposals.novelty: While version control is standard in software development and emerging in computational science, its structured application to the human decision-making processes within systematic reviews is novel. This approach moves beyond simply archiving final data (as surveyed in Moher's prior work) to capturing the dynamic evolution of the review itself.

proposals.title: Developing and pilot-testing an Agile Systematic Review (ASR) framework: a protocol and feasibility study
proposals.motivation: Systematic reviews are critical for evidence-based medicine but are often slow and resource-intensive, limiting their timeliness for urgent policy and clinical decisions. Traditional linear review conduct, as codified in PRISMA and Cochrane guidelines, can be inflexible, with stakeholder feedback typically occurring only at protocol and final draft stages. This delay risks producing reviews that are outdated or misaligned with end-user needs upon completion.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying core agile project management principles—specifically iterative sprints, continuous stakeholder engagement, and adaptive planning—to systematic review conduct will reduce time-to-completion and increase the relevance and utility of the final review product without compromising methodological rigor.
proposals.method: We will develop a formal Agile Systematic Review (ASR) framework by adapting Scrum methodologies to review stages (e.g., sprint 1: search strategy refinement; sprint 2: pilot data extraction). The framework will be piloted in three concurrent systematic reviews. Feasibility and impact will be assessed using a mixed-methods approach: quantitative metrics (time per stage, protocol amendments) and qualitative interviews with review team members and stakeholders. The framework's integrity will be evaluated against core PRISMA reporting items to ensure methodological standards are maintained.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, structured protocol for agile review conduct, filling a gap in methodological guidance focused on process management. It will provide empirical, pre-2026 evidence on the feasibility, efficiency, and perceived value of iterative, stakeholder-informed review production, contributing directly to the 'rapid review' and research-on-research literature.
proposals.novelty: While rapid review methods exist, the explicit, formalized translation of agile project management from software development to the entire systematic review lifecycle, with a focus on continuous iteration and stakeholder feedback loops, is a novel methodological innovation not previously codified or tested.

proposals.title: Developing and Testing User-Persona-Specific Reporting Templates for Systematic Reviews: A Mixed-Methods Study
proposals.motivation: Systematic review reports, while guided by standards like PRISMA, are often monolithic documents designed for researchers. This one-size-fits-all approach fails to meet the distinct information needs and decision-making contexts of key end-users like clinicians, policymakers, and patients. This gap limits the uptake and real-world impact of systematic review evidence, a persistent problem in knowledge translation identified in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying user experience (UX) design principles to create and test systematic review report formats tailored to specific user personas (clinician, policymaker, patient) will improve end-users' comprehension, perceived usefulness, and efficiency in finding key information compared to a standard full-text report.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase study. First, a scoping review and qualitative interviews will define the core information needs and format preferences for each persona. Second, using an iterative, participatory design process involving representatives from each user group, we will co-create prototype report templates (e.g., a one-page clinical decision aid, a policy brief with graded evidence, a patient-friendly summary). Third, we will conduct a randomized experiment (using a between-subjects design) where participants from each persona group are assigned to receive either a standard review or the persona-specific version, assessing outcomes via validated tests of comprehension, usability surveys, and time-to-find-key-information tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, empirically-derived framework for persona-driven reporting in evidence synthesis, moving beyond a single reporting checklist. It will yield practical, tested template prototypes that review authors and journals can adopt, directly addressing calls for more accessible and usable research outputs. The findings will advance the field of research reporting by integrating UX methodology into guideline development.
proposals.novelty: While CONSORT and PRISMA extensions exist for different review types, the application of formal UX design and persona-based co-creation to tailor the *same* review's output for different audiences is novel. This shifts the focus from standardized reporting for completeness to user-centered design for optimal uptake and utility.

proposals.title: Detecting Outcome Reporting Bias in Meta-Analyses: An Application of P-Hacking Detection Methods from Psychology
proposals.motivation: Outcome reporting bias (ORB), where studies selectively report outcomes based on their results, is a major threat to the validity of systematic reviews and meta-analyses, distorting the evidence base for clinical and policy decisions. Pre-2026, methods to detect ORB are limited, often relying on comparing published protocols to final reports, which are frequently unavailable. This leaves a critical gap for assessing bias in the vast body of existing meta-analyses where protocols do not exist.
proposals.hypothesis: Statistical methods developed to detect p-hacking in collections of primary studies (e.g., excess of p-values just below 0.05) can be adapted and applied to the distribution of effect sizes or p-values reported across studies within a meta-analysis to serve as indicators of selective outcome reporting at the synthesis level.
proposals.method: We will adapt and apply established p-hacking detection tests, such as the p-curve analysis and the test of excess significance (TES), to the body of studies included in a sample of published meta-analyses. The approach involves: 1) Extracting the primary outcome p-values or effect sizes from each study within selected meta-analyses from diverse biomedical fields. 2) Applying the p-curve analysis to assess if the distribution of p-values is consistent with selective reporting. 3) Using the TES to compare the observed number of statistically significant studies to the expected number based on the average power of the included studies. We will validate findings against a gold-standard set of meta-analyses with known ORB, identified via published protocols (e.g., from Cochrane reviews).
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a novel, statistical screening tool to flag meta-analyses at high risk of outcome reporting bias, complementing existing guideline-based approaches like PRISMA. It will provide systematic reviewers and journal editors with a practical method to assess the robustness of synthesized evidence, even in the absence of protocols, thereby improving research integrity and the reliability of evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While p-hacking detection methods are established in psychology for primary studies, their direct application to diagnose bias within the synthesized results of a meta-analysis is novel. This transposes a method for detecting bias in data generation to a method for detecting bias in evidence synthesis, a logical but unexplored extension given Moher's expertise in reporting guidelines and meta-research.

proposals.title: Developing and piloting a framework for assessing risk of bias in complex health services and delivery research (HSDR) systematic reviews: an adaptation of methods from social intervention reviews
proposals.motivation: Health services and delivery research (HSDR) systematically reviews face significant challenges in bias assessment due to the inherent heterogeneity of interventions, contexts, and study designs, which are not adequately addressed by existing tools like Cochrane's RoB 2.0. This methodological gap threatens the validity of evidence synthesis in a field critical for informing healthcare policy and practice. Adapting established methods from complex social intervention reviews, which routinely handle such heterogeneity, offers a promising but untested solution.
proposals.hypothesis: A bias assessment framework adapted from complex social intervention review methods (e.g., from the Campbell Collaboration) will be more applicable, feasible, and reliable for use in HSDR systematic reviews than current domain-based tools, leading to more nuanced and accurate judgments of evidence certainty.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, consensus-based project. First, a scoping review will identify and map bias assessment methods from complex social intervention reviews. Second, an international Delphi study with HSDR methodologies, systematic reviewers, and content experts will adapt and refine a draft framework. Third, we will perform an inter-rater reliability study, comparing the new framework against RoB 2.0 in a sample of 20-30 HSDR studies, using Cohen's kappa and intraclass correlation coefficients to assess agreement.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a pilot-tested, consensus-based framework specifically designed for bias assessment in complex HSDR reviews, filling a critical methodological gap. It will provide concrete guidelines and reporting standards (extending CONSORT and PRISMA principles) to improve the rigor and transparency of evidence synthesis in health services research.
proposals.novelty: While bias tools exist, this work uniquely translates and validates methodological approaches from a distinct but analogous evidence synthesis field (social interventions) to address the specific complexities of HSDR, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all application of clinical trial assessment tools.

proposals.title: Development and initial validation of the Rapid Review Bias Assessment Tool (RR-BAT): a pragmatic tool for assessing publication bias in rapid health services and delivery research reviews
proposals.motivation: Rapid reviews are essential for timely health services and delivery research (HSDR) decision-making, but their expedited nature risks compromising methodological rigor, particularly in assessing publication bias. Existing tools like the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool or extensive funnel plot analyses are too resource-intensive for rapid review timelines. There is a critical gap for a validated, minimal tool that allows rapid review teams to conduct a pragmatic, yet systematic, assessment of publication bias risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal, domain-adapted tool for assessing publication bias in rapid HSDR reviews can be developed with acceptable inter-rater reliability and perceived usability among systematic reviewers, without significantly extending review timelines.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, consensus-based methodology. First, a scoping review and expert survey (using Delphi methods with the CONSORT and PRISMA groups) will identify core items from existing bias assessment frameworks critical for HSDR. Second, these items will be distilled into a ≤5-item checklist (RR-BAT) through iterative rounds of stakeholder feedback. Third, we will conduct a replication-style test, asking rapid review teams to apply both the RR-BAT and a standard tool to a sample of HSDR reviews, comparing time-to-completion and inter-rater reliability (using Cohen's kappa). Usability will be assessed via a validated survey.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formally developed, pragmatic tool specifically for publication bias assessment in rapid HSDR reviews. It will provide methodological guidance for rapid reviewers and contribute to improving the transparency and credibility of rapid review evidence used by policymakers. The tool and its development process will serve as a model for creating other scaled-down methodological instruments.
proposals.novelty: While checklists exist for full systematic reviews, creating a validated *minimal* tool for rapid reviews represents a novel application of methodological scaling principles. The focus on HSDR—a field with diverse study designs beyond RCTs—and the integration of usability testing directly into the tool's development process moves beyond simply shortening existing lists.

proposals.title: Development and validation of a natural language processing algorithm for the automated, large-scale identification of systematic reviews at high risk of bias
proposals.motivation: The exponential growth of systematic reviews (SRs) has strained the capacity of journal editors, peer reviewers, and evidence synthesis groups to manually assess methodological rigor. High risk of bias (RoB) in SRs undermines the reliability of evidence used for clinical and policy decisions. Pre-2026, tools like ROBIS exist for manual assessment, but no scalable, automated solution exists to triage reviews in large databases for urgent manual checking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised machine learning algorithm, trained on features extracted from the text and reporting patterns of SRs with known ROBIS assessments, can accurately classify SRs as having a 'high' or 'low' risk of bias, enabling efficient database-scale screening.
proposals.method: We will develop a labeled dataset of SRs with expert-applied ROBIS domain judgments. Using natural language processing (NLP), we will extract features from the full text, including adherence to PRISMA reporting guidelines (via text pattern recognition), statistical reporting completeness, and linguistic markers of uncertainty. We will train and compare multiple classifier architectures, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO) and ensemble methods (Random Forests), to predict overall RoB. The final algorithm will be validated on a held-out sample and tested for deployment feasibility in databases like PubMed via automated metadata and abstract screening.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source algorithm to automatically flag SRs likely requiring urgent methodological scrutiny, acting as a 'triage' tool for evidence custodians. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for applying reporting guideline adherence (e.g., PRISMA) as a machine-readable proxy for RoB assessment at scale. The resulting tool will directly support the replication and robustness of evidence synthesis by prioritizing manual evaluation efforts.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on manual RoB tools or automated screening for study inclusion, this project innovates by directly translating the ROBIS framework—a consensus-based, domain-specific tool—into an automated classifier for entire systematic reviews, leveraging both reporting completeness and narrative text analysis.

proposals.title: Predicting Systematic Review Methodological Quality from the Reporting of Bias Assessments: A Machine Learning Analysis
proposals.motivation: The methodological quality of systematic reviews is critical for evidence-based medicine, but it is labor-intensive to assess using tools like AMSTAR-2 or ROBIS. Pre-2026 research, including CONSORT and PRISMA extensions, emphasizes transparent reporting of bias assessments. This study investigates whether simpler, machine-detectable signals in review text, specifically the mention of bias, can serve as a rapid, automated proxy for overall quality, potentially aiding in the rapid identification of reviews requiring deeper scrutiny.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the explicit mention of bias-related terms and concepts (e.g., 'selection bias', 'risk of bias assessment', 'publication bias') within the full text of a systematic review is a positive predictor of its overall methodological quality score.
proposals.method: We will assemble a corpus of published biomedical systematic reviews with pre-existing, independent AMSTAR-2 quality assessments. Using natural language processing (NLP), we will extract features including the presence, frequency, and context of a pre-defined lexicon of bias terms derived from reporting guidelines. We will train and compare multiple supervised machine learning models (e.g., logistic regression, random forests, and support vector machines) to predict a binary quality outcome (high/low) based on these textual features, using cross-validation and reporting performance metrics like AUC-ROC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the relationship between specific reporting practices and methodological quality. It will contribute a potential framework for the rapid, automated screening of review quality, complementing existing labor-intensive tools. The findings could inform future iterations of reporting guidelines and journal peer-review processes.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has analyzed reporting completeness, this study inverts the problem by using a single, focused reporting element (bias mention) to predict a global quality score. It applies NLP and machine learning techniques common in other fields to a novel question in research synthesis methodology, moving beyond correlation to predictive modeling.

proposals.title: A Mixed-Methods Study of Systematic Reviewer Characteristics and Training as Predictors of Risk-of-Bias Assessment Propensity and Consistency
proposals.motivation: Risk-of-bias (RoB) assessment is a cornerstone of systematic reviews, yet its reliability is often poor. Pre-2026 research has focused on the performance of RoB tools (e.g., Cochrane RoB 1 & 2, ROBINS-I) but has largely overlooked the characteristics and training of the reviewers applying them. Understanding how reviewer expertise, methodological background, and training influence their propensity to identify and report bias is critical for improving review quality and replicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic reviewers' propensity and consistency in assessing risk of bias are significantly associated with specific characteristics, including formal training in epidemiology/biostatistics, years of review experience, and type of training (e.g., completion of formal courses vs. on-the-job learning).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional survey of corresponding authors of systematic reviews published in the last two years, identified via PubMed. The survey will collect data on demographics, training, experience, and self-reported practices. In a linked experimental component, a subset of respondents will assess the risk of bias in a standard set of 3-5 study excerpts using a specified tool (e.g., RoB 2). We will use multivariable regression to model the association between reviewer characteristics and: 1) self-reported thoroughness of RoB assessment (survey), and 2) consistency (inter-rater reliability) and accuracy (against a pre-defined expert consensus) in the experimental task.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence linking specific reviewer attributes to RoB assessment behaviors. It will generate actionable insights for developing more effective training programs and guidelines for conducting RoB assessments, ultimately aiming to standardize and improve this critical component of evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined data sharing and reporting guidelines (Moher's recent papers), this proposal shifts focus to the reviewers themselves as a potential source of variability. It moves beyond tool development to investigate the human factors that influence the application of existing tools, a less-studied determinant of review quality.

proposals.title: Shifting the Gatekeepers: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Reporting Guideline Intervention Targeting Peer Reviewers and Editors to Improve Manuscript Compliance
proposals.motivation: Despite the widespread development of reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT, PRISMA), adherence in published manuscripts remains suboptimal. Pre-2026 evidence suggests that authors are often unaware of or do not fully apply these standards. The current paradigm primarily targets authors, but peer reviewers and editors are the critical gatekeepers who assess manuscript quality and enforce journal policies. This study addresses the hypothesis that shifting the primary intervention target to these gatekeepers may be more effective.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing peer reviewers and editors with a structured, checklist-based tool derived from reporting guidelines, alongside a brief training module, will lead to a greater improvement in manuscript reporting completeness compared to providing the same materials directly to authors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, parallel-group randomized controlled trial. Manuscripts submitted to participating biomedical journals will be cluster-randomized by manuscript to one of three arms: (1) Intervention for Reviewers/Editors: Reviewers and handling editors receive a tailored checklist (based on CONSORT or PRISMA, as appropriate) and a short online training video on its use; (2) Intervention for Authors: Corresponding authors receive the same checklist and training; (3) Control: Usual peer review process. The primary outcome is the change in adherence score (0-100%) on the relevant reporting guideline, assessed by comparing the initially submitted manuscript to the final accepted version using blinded raters. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat, linear mixed-effects model.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence on the comparative effectiveness of targeting different stakeholders with reporting guideline interventions. It will generate practical knowledge for journal editors, guideline developers, and research integrity networks on how to operationalize guidelines within the peer review workflow to maximize their impact on published research quality.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on author education or journal endorsement, this trial directly tests the novel strategy of empowering reviewers and editors as the primary agents for enforcing reporting standards, a conceptual shift from the prevailing author-centric implementation model.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal, Multi-Journal Evaluation of the Impact of STROBE Statement Endorsement on the Completeness of Observational Study Reporting
proposals.motivation: The STROBE Statement, established to improve the reporting of observational studies, has been widely endorsed by journals. However, its actual impact on the completeness of published reports remains inadequately measured. Given the critical role of transparent reporting in assessing risk of bias, facilitating replication, and informing evidence synthesis, an empirical evaluation of STROBE's effectiveness is urgently needed to guide journal policy and author instruction.
proposals.hypothesis: The formal endorsement of the STROBE Statement by biomedical journals is associated with a statistically significant increase in the completeness of reporting for key methodological and results items in published cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled, longitudinal study using a quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series design. We will systematically sample observational studies published in a cohort of journals before and after their formal adoption of STROBE, matched with a control cohort of non-adopting journals. Reporting completeness will be assessed using a pre-specified, piloted data extraction form based on the 22-item STROBE checklist. Trained, independent extractors, blinded to the journal's STROBE endorsement status, will score each item. We will use generalized linear mixed models to compare mean completeness scores and adherence to critical items (e.g., confounding, bias, study size justification) between pre- and post-adoption periods, adjusting for journal, study design, and year.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, controlled evidence on the real-world effectiveness of the STROBE Statement as a journal policy intervention. The findings will directly inform journal editors, publishers, and guideline developers about the value of mandating reporting guidelines and may identify specific items where reporting remains deficient, guiding future iterations of STROBE and targeted educational efforts.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have described reporting quality, this proposal uses a robust, controlled design to isolate the effect of STROBE endorsement itself, moving beyond simple before-after comparisons. It also leverages Moher's expertise in guideline development (CONSORT) and data sharing to ensure a methodologically rigorous and transparent evaluation.

proposals.title: Developing a reporting guideline for observational studies using nationwide registries and other massive datasets: The MASS-OBS (Massive Observational Studies Standards) project
proposals.motivation: The use of nationwide registries, electronic health records, and other 'big data' sources for observational research is rapidly expanding, offering unprecedented scale.
