However, existing reporting guidelines like STROBE are not fully equipped to address the unique methodological and transparency challenges of these massive datasets, such as data linkage processes, complex confounding control, and computational reproducibility. This gap threatens the validity, interpretability, and utility of a growing body of evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: A consensus-based, domain-specific reporting guideline for massive observational datasets will improve the completeness, transparency, and reproducibility of published research using these sources, compared to the current application of generic observational study guidelines.
proposals.method: The project will follow established guideline development methodology, akin to CONSORT and STROBE. We will conduct: 1) a systematic scoping review to identify reported challenges and existing checklists; 2) an online Delphi survey with three rounds involving an international panel of epidemiologists, data scientists, journal editors, and patient partners to build consensus on essential reporting items; and 3) a consensus meeting to finalize the guideline and its explanation and elaboration document. The process will be managed using standard Delphi techniques and facilitated via a dedicated online platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a freely available, consensus-based reporting guideline (MASS-OBS) with an accompanying explanation and elaboration paper. It will directly address gaps in reporting for data linkage, provenance, computational workflows, and bias assessment specific to massive datasets. This will provide a critical tool for authors, peer reviewers, and journals to enhance research quality and trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While extensions of STROBE exist for specific designs, no guideline has been developed through formal consensus specifically for the unique paradigm of pre-existing, population-scale, administratively collected datasets where the unit of analysis is often the data point itself, not the recruited participant. This project moves beyond adapting existing frameworks to creating a fit-for-purpose standard.

proposals.title: A Natural Language Processing System to Infer Missing Observational Study Design Elements from Published Reports: A Development and Validation Study
proposals.motivation: Incomplete reporting of observational study methods, such as participant selection criteria, exposure/outcome definitions, and confounding control, is a major barrier to evidence synthesis and replication. Pre-2026, reporting guidelines like STROBE have improved but not solved this problem, and systematic reviewers spend considerable time manually contacting authors for missing details. An automated tool to infer these elements could significantly accelerate evidence reviews and improve the utility of published literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based natural language processing model, trained on a corpus of fully reported observational studies, can accurately infer key missing methodological details from the text of incomplete study reports.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised learning model using a BERT-based architecture (e.g., BioBERT or SciBERT) fine-tuned for multi-label classification and sequence tagging. The training dataset will be constructed from a curated sample of observational studies from PubMed, annotated against the STROBE checklist. The model will be trained to predict the presence and description of specific design elements (e.g., study setting, eligibility criteria, statistical methods for control of confounding) from the manuscript's Methods and Results sections. Performance will be validated on a held-out test set and benchmarked against human annotators.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source tool to assist systematic reviewers and meta-analysts in rapidly extracting and standardizing methodological data from observational studies. It will contribute a novel dataset of annotated study reports and provide empirical evidence on the feasibility of automating the inference of study design, a core task in evidence-based medicine.
proposals.novelty: While existing NLP tools extract reported items, this project directly addresses the 'inverse problem' of inferring *unreported* methodological details, moving beyond information extraction to methodological gap-filling. It integrates Moher's expertise in reporting guidelines with modern NLP, applying it to the specific challenge of incomplete observational study reports, which are prevalent in the literature.

proposals.title: Developing a dynamic reporting guideline for longitudinal observational studies: A consensus and replication-based project
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal studies are critical for understanding health trajectories but are often reported using the static STROBE checklist, which is ill-suited for their ongoing, multi-phase nature. This leads to fragmented reporting, hindering the synthesis of evidence across timepoints and reducing the utility of data for future research. A guideline for dynamic, iterative reporting is needed to improve transparency, reproducibility, and data sharing in longitudinal research.
proposals.hypothesis: A consensus-based, dynamic extension of the STROBE guideline, developed through a formal guideline development process and tested via replication exercises, will improve the completeness and usability of reporting for multi-phase longitudinal observational studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage project. First, a scoping review will map existing reporting challenges and solutions for longitudinal studies. Second, a multi-round Delphi survey involving methodologies, longitudinal study leads, journal editors, and statisticians will build consensus on candidate items. Third, a consensus meeting (in-person or virtual) will finalize the guideline structure, which will incorporate mechanisms for iterative reporting (e.g., versioned reports, linked data repositories). Finally, we will test the guideline's utility by applying it to replicate the reporting of a sample of published longitudinal studies and assess improvements in reporting completeness.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formal, consensus-based reporting guideline extension for dynamic, longitudinal observational studies. It will provide a practical framework for researchers to report evolving studies transparently, enhance the ability to synthesize longitudinal evidence, and promote data sharing across study phases. The work will directly extend Moher's expertise in CONSORT and guideline development into the critical domain of longitudinal research.
proposals.novelty: While STROBE extensions exist for specific fields, none address the fundamental temporal and iterative nature of longitudinal studies. This project innovates by moving from a static checklist model to a dynamic reporting framework, explicitly designed for studies that evolve and release findings over time, integrating concepts of versioning and ongoing data disclosure.

proposals.title: Assessing the Decoupling of Reporting Completeness from Methodological Conduct: A Multi-Method Study of Clinical Trials Published Post-CONSORT
proposals.motivation: Reporting guidelines like CONSORT are widely endorsed to improve transparency and reduce bias. However, a critical, unexamined assumption is that better reporting reflects or drives better methodological conduct. There is a risk that these guidelines incentivize a 'box-ticking' compliance where reports appear complete but underlying study rigor, such as proper randomization or blinding implementation, remains unchanged or even deteriorates, creating a false sense of trustworthiness in the literature.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption of reporting guidelines has led to a decoupling between the completeness of a trial's published report and the actual methodological quality of its conduct, as assessed through independent verification or detailed manuscript scrutiny.
proposals.method: We will conduct a replication-based cohort study comparing trials published before and after the widespread adoption of CONSORT (e.g., 1995-2005 vs. 2015-2025). For a random sample of trials from high-impact journals, we will: 1) Use automated text mining (natural language processing) to score adherence to CONSORT checklist items from published manuscripts. 2) Employ two blinded methodologies to independently appraise the methodological conduct quality using tools like the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0, focusing on implementation details beyond mere reporting. 3) Statistically model the relationship between reporting scores and conduct scores across time periods, controlling for journal, funder, and topic. A significant weakening of this correlation in the later period would support the hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence testing the foundational assumption linking reporting and conduct. It will inform the future development of guidelines and journal policies, potentially arguing for more integrated conduct-and-reporting standards or verification mechanisms. The findings will be crucial for systematic reviewers, meta-analysts, and clinicians who rely on published trial reports to judge evidence trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has evaluated reporting guideline adherence, no study has explicitly tested for a growing divergence between reported and actual methodological quality as a potential unintended consequence of these guidelines. The application of replication principles and text mining to this specific question is novel.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Systematic Review Protocols from Published Reports: A Natural Language Processing Approach to Enhance Transparency and Reproducibility
proposals.motivation: Systematic review protocols are essential for transparency and to reduce bias, yet they are often unpublished or poorly reported. This creates a 'reverse reporting gap,' hindering replication, updating of reviews, and assessment of selective reporting. An automated method to infer the protocol from the final publication would provide a crucial check on review conduct and facilitate the sharing of review methods as data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key elements of a systematic review protocol—including PICO (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome) criteria, search strategy, and eligibility rules—can be accurately extracted and reconstructed from the methods and results sections of a published systematic review using supervised natural language processing models.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using pre-2026 transformer-based architectures (e.g., BERT, SciBERT) fine-tuned on a manually annotated corpus of systematic review pairs (published protocols and their corresponding final reports). The model will be trained to perform named entity recognition and relation extraction for protocol elements. Performance will be validated against a held-out test set and evaluated using standard metrics (precision, recall, F1-score). We will also conduct a feasibility study applying the tool to a sample of reviews from the researcher's prior survey on data sharing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source tool to audit the internal consistency and completeness of published systematic reviews. It will contribute directly to the CONSORT and PRISMA ecosystems by providing an automated method to assess protocol adherence. The resulting 'inferred protocol' dataset could serve as a resource for methodological research on reporting practices.
proposals.novelty: While existing NLP tools extract PICO elements from primary studies, applying this to reconstruct the *design* of a secondary study (the systematic review itself) from its report is a novel inverse problem. It shifts the focus from aiding literature search to auditing review conduct and transparency.

proposals.title: Inter-Rater Variability in the Interpretation and Implementation of TIDieR-Compliant Intervention Descriptions: A Mixed-Methods Replication Study
proposals.motivation: The Template for Intervention Description and Replication (TIDieR) checklist is a key reporting guideline designed to improve the completeness of intervention descriptions. However, pre-2026 research, including Moher's work on reporting and replication, has focused primarily on author adherence to reporting standards, not on how readers interpret reported information. This gap is critical because variability in interpretation by systematic reviewers, clinicians, and trialists can lead to inconsistent implementation and synthesis, undermining replication and evidence-based practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that even among expert systematic reviewers, there will be significant variability in the interpretation and proposed operationalization of key intervention components from TIDieR-compliant descriptions, and that this variability will be associated with reviewer experience and domain expertise.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods, cross-sectional study using a replication framework. We will purposively sample 30-40 systematic reviewers with experience in complementary and integrative medicine (CIM). Participants will be presented with 3-5 published, TIDieR-compliant CIM intervention descriptions. Using a structured online survey (including free-text and multiple-choice questions), we will ask them to interpret and specify implementation details for ambiguous components (e.g., 'dose,' 'tailoring'). We will quantify inter-rater agreement using Fleiss' kappa for categorical items and analyze qualitative free-text responses using thematic analysis to identify sources of interpretive divergence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence quantifying interpretive variability in using TIDieR, moving the field beyond a focus on reporting completeness. It will generate concrete recommendations for enhancing TIDieR guidance to reduce ambiguity. The findings will directly inform the development of more robust reporting and synthesis practices, ultimately improving research replicability.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional focus from checking author compliance with reporting guidelines to studying reader interpretation—a critical but neglected link in the replication chain. It applies a replication science lens to the problem of guideline implementation, a novel synthesis of Moher's core themes.

proposals.title: Developing a CONSORT Extension for Adaptive Interventions (CONSORT-AI): A Delphi Study and Reporting Guideline
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, there is a proliferation of adaptive interventions (e.g., in digital health, behavioral medicine, and precision public health) where treatment components, dosage, or delivery are intentionally modified based on participant response or context. Existing reporting guidelines like CONSORT are designed for fixed-protocol trials and fail to capture the essential decision rules, adaptation algorithms, and implementation fidelity of these dynamic interventions, hindering replication, synthesis, and clinical application.
proposals.hypothesis: A formal consensus process involving key stakeholders (trialists, methodologies, journal editors, and patient partners) will yield a core set of essential reporting items for adaptive intervention trials that, when adopted, will improve the completeness, transparency, and reproducibility of such research.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage modified Delphi study. First, a systematic review and expert interviews will generate a preliminary list of candidate reporting items. Second, an international, multi-stakeholder panel (n=~30) will complete iterative online Delphi rounds to rate the importance of each item. Third, a consensus meeting will finalize the checklist and explanatory document. The guideline development will follow the EQUATOR Network methodology, analogous to the development of CONSORT extensions for non-pharmacologic treatments or pragmatic trials.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first formal, consensus-based reporting guideline (CONSORT-AI) specifically for trials of adaptive interventions. It will provide a critical tool for authors, peer reviewers, and editors to improve reporting quality. This will directly enhance the interpretability, replicability, and utility of a growing class of complex health interventions.
proposals.novelty: While CONSORT extensions exist for various designs, none address the core reporting challenge of dynamic, algorithm-driven interventions where the protocol is not fixed. This work moves beyond static reporting frameworks to create a standard for documenting the logic and flow of adaptive sequences.

proposals.title: Development and Consensus on TIDieR Extensions for Non-Randomized Evaluative Study Designs: A Delphi Study and Pilot Test
proposals.motivation: The TIDieR (Template for Intervention Description and Replication) checklist, published in 2014, is a critical tool for improving the reporting of interventions in randomized trials. However, its applicability to other evaluative study designs (e.g., quasi-experimental, single-case, adaptive trials) is limited, as these designs have unique methodological features and reporting needs. This gap hinders the replication, synthesis, and application of evidence from a broad spectrum of intervention research, a core concern in the reproducibility movement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that developing and validating tailored TIDieR extensions for specific non-randomized evaluative study designs will significantly improve the completeness of intervention description in published reports of these studies compared to the use of the original TIDieR checklist alone.
proposals.method: This project will employ a multi-phase consensus and validation approach. First, we will conduct a scoping review to identify reporting challenges for interventions within specific designs (e.g., interrupted time series, stepped-wedge). Second, using a modified Delphi process with an international panel of methodologies, journal editors, and funders, we will draft design-specific TIDieR extensions. Third, we will pilot test the new extensions by applying them to a sample of published and unpublished protocols and reports, using the CONSORT adaptation framework to assess feasibility and potential impact on reporting completeness.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a suite of validated, consensus-based reporting extensions to the TIDieR checklist, directly addressing a known gap in the reporting guideline ecosystem. It will enhance the replicability and utility of intervention research beyond RCTs, supporting better evidence synthesis and clinical application. The project will also provide a methodological template for adapting other core reporting guidelines to specific contexts.
proposals.novelty: While TIDieR is widely endorsed, no formal process has been undertaken to adapt it for the distinct reporting requirements of quasi-experimental and other complex evaluative designs, moving beyond its original trial-centric focus. This project integrates Moher's expertise in guideline development (CONSORT) with the specific need for detailed intervention reporting across all evidence-generating study types.

proposals.title: Development and Usability Testing of an Interactive Web-Based Platform for Adherence to the TIDieR (Template for Intervention Description and Replication) Checklist
proposals.motivation: Inadequate reporting of interventions remains a major barrier to research replication, synthesis, and clinical application. The TIDieR checklist, an extension of CONSORT and SPIRIT, was developed to improve this, but adherence is inconsistent. Digital tools that actively guide reporting, based on successful models for other reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT), could significantly enhance the completeness and usability of intervention descriptions.
proposals.hypothesis: An interactive, web-based platform that structures the reporting process according to the TIDieR checklist will produce more complete and replicable intervention descriptions from researchers, compared to using a static PDF checklist, and will be perceived as more usable and helpful.
proposals.method: We will employ a user-centered design and mixed-methods approach. First, we will conduct stakeholder interviews to define requirements. The platform will be developed as a dynamic web application using a React.js frontend and a Node.js backend. It will implement a rule-based engine to provide context-sensitive guidance, logic checks (e.g., flagging inconsistencies), and prompts for each TIDieR item. We will then conduct a randomized controlled experiment, comparing the completeness of intervention descriptions written using the platform versus the standard PDF checklist, followed by validated usability testing (System Usability Scale) and qualitative feedback.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce a freely available, validated digital tool to improve intervention reporting fidelity, directly supporting replication efforts. It will also generate novel empirical evidence on the effectiveness of interactive digital aids for reporting guideline implementation, extending Dr. Moher's work on data sharing and guideline development into the digital toolspace.
proposals.novelty: While static TIDieR templates exist, this project creates an intelligent, interactive system with built-in logic and guidance, moving beyond a passive PDF. It integrates usability science and experimental evaluation directly into the development of a reporting tool, a method not yet widely applied to TIDieR.

proposals.title: Developing and validating a TIDieR-based template for structuring implementation guides in clinical practice guidelines: a Delphi consensus study and usability test
proposals.motivation: The Template for Intervention Description and Replication (TIDieR) checklist is a critical tool for improving the reporting of interventions in research. However, its structured format is not directly translated into practical templates for guideline developers and implementers, leading to inconsistent and incomplete implementation guidance in published clinical practice guidelines. This gap hinders the effective adoption of evidence-based interventions in real-world settings, a persistent problem in knowledge translation.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the core TIDieR checklist items into a structured, user-friendly template for guideline implementation manuals will improve their completeness, clarity, and perceived usability by end-users (e.g., clinicians, administrators) compared to current ad-hoc formats.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase, consensus-based methodology. First, a steering committee will draft an initial template by mapping TIDieR items to the needs of implementation guides. Second, we will conduct a three-round modified Delphi study with a purposive panel of international experts in guideline development, implementation science, and clinical practice to refine and achieve consensus on template items and structure. Finally, we will perform a usability test with guideline developers and end-users, employing think-aloud protocols and validated usability scales (e.g., System Usability Scale) to evaluate the template's practicality.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, consensus-based template specifically designed for crafting implementation guides within clinical practice guidelines, directly extending the reach of TIDieR from research reporting to practical application. It will contribute a methodological framework for adapting reporting guidelines into implementation tools and provide empirical evidence on the template's usability to bridge the evidence-practice gap.
proposals.novelty: While TIDieR is widely endorsed for research reporting, its systematic translation into a ready-to-use template for non-academic implementation manuals is novel. This project moves beyond reporting *what* was done in a study to prescribing *how* to structure guidance for doing it in practice, a logical but unexplored extension of Moher's work on guidelines and reporting.

proposals.title: The FORMAT-BIAS Study: A Crossover Trial to Quantify the Influence of Protocol Template Structure on Expert Judgments of Scientific Merit
proposals.motivation: Reporting guidelines like SPIRIT and CONSORT aim to improve research quality and transparency by standardizing protocol and manuscript structure. However, the potential for these formats to introduce systematic bias in expert evaluation—where presentation influences the perception of substance—remains unmeasured. This is critical as such bias could undermine the fairness of peer review and grant funding decisions, privileging form over scientific content.
proposals.hypothesis: The standardized format of a research protocol systematically influences expert reviewers' scores for scientific rigor, feasibility, and clarity, independent of the underlying scientific content, with highly structured formats inducing a positive bias compared to minimally structured presentations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, crossover study with experienced systematic review and trial methodologies as participants. Each reviewer will assess three versions of the same core protocol content, presented in distinct formats: 1) a highly standardized SPIRIT/PRISMA-P template, 2) a narrative, minimally structured document, and 3) a hybrid format. Using validated scales, we will measure scores for rigor, feasibility, and clarity. The primary analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to isolate the variance attributable to format, controlling for reviewer and protocol content, with post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey's HSD) comparing formats.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence quantifying format-induced bias in protocol assessment. The results will inform the development of an evidence-based framework for template design, helping organizations like EQUATOR refine guidelines to minimize bias and support, rather than inadvertently supplant, critical appraisal of scientific substance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Moher and others has focused on developing and promoting reporting guidelines, this study directly tests an adversarial hypothesis—that standardization can introduce bias—using a rigorous experimental design to decouple presentation from content, a question not addressed in existing guideline literature.

proposals.title: Adapting Aviation Safety Verification Checklists to Improve the Reporting and Operational Integrity of Clinical Trial Protocols: A Feasibility Study
proposals.motivation: Poorly designed and reported clinical trial protocols lead to research waste, ethical concerns, and unreliable evidence. Existing reporting guidelines like SPIRIT improve protocol content but focus on documentation rather than active verification of operational safety and feasibility. High-reliability industries like aviation use rigorous, iterative checklist systems to prevent failures before they occur.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically adapting the structure and verification processes of aviation safety documents (e.g., FAA AC 25.1309-1B for system design) into a checklist for clinical trial protocols will identify a greater number of critical operational and safety flaws during the protocol development stage compared to standard SPIRIT-guided review.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-phase study. First, a mapping exercise will identify analogous concepts between aviation safety assurance (e.g., failure modes, redundancy, crew resource management) and clinical trial conduct. Second, we will develop a draft 'Protocol Operational Safety Checklist' (POSC) by adapting specific items from aviation documents like the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and pre-flight checklists. Third, we will perform a feasibility test using a comparative case study: two independent methodological experts will apply both the POSC and the SPIRIT checklist to a sample of 10 publicly available trial protocols, quantifying and categorizing the unique flaws identified by each tool.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operationally-focused verification tool for trial protocols, moving beyond reporting completeness to proactive risk mitigation. It will provide preliminary evidence on whether cross-industry safety engineering methods can enhance clinical research integrity and reduce protocol amendments.
proposals.novelty: While checklists exist in clinical research (e.g., CONSORT, SPIRIT), directly translating the iterative, failure-mode-based verification culture from ultra-high-stakes regulatory environments like aviation to trial protocol design is a novel, untested approach. It shifts the focus from 'what to report' to 'how to verify operational robustness before the trial begins.'

proposals.title: A Logic-Based System for Automated Protocol Generation and Consistency Checking from Clinical Trial Registry Data
proposals.motivation: Incomplete and inconsistent reporting of trial protocols in public registries like ClinicalTrials.gov undermines research transparency, reproducibility, and the utility of systematic reviews. Pre-2026 work, including CONSORT and SPIRIT guidelines, has focused on manual reporting standards, but automated tools to ensure logical consistency between a trial's design elements are lacking. This gap contributes to research waste and limits the secondary use of registry data for evidence synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that reverse-engineers a structured protocol draft from registry entries and applies formal logic checking can reliably identify and propose repairs for logical inconsistencies within the protocol and the source registry data.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using Natural Language Processing (NLP) to extract and structure key protocol elements (e.g., eligibility, interventions, endpoints) from ClinicalTrials.gov entries. This structured draft will be converted into a formal model using the Alloy specification language. We will define a set of logical constraints (e.g., an endpoint must be measurable given the intervention) based on SPIRIT and methodological principles. The Alloy Analyzer will perform model checking to identify violations, and a repair module will generate specific, minimal edits to resolve inconsistencies, either in the generated protocol or by flagging the source registry field.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source tool that enhances the logical integrity of trial protocols at the registration stage. It contributes to the field of research reporting by moving from static guidelines to dynamic, computational compliance checking, directly supporting systematic reviewers and trial registries in improving data quality.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on reporting completeness, this system uniquely integrates NLP-based protocol generation with formal methods for logical consistency checking, creating a bidirectional feedback loop between protocol drafts and registry data quality.

proposals.title: A semi-automated, evidence-anchored system for clinical trial protocol generation and discrepancy detection: a development and validation study
proposals.motivation: Clinical trial registries are often sparse, lacking the methodological detail required for rigorous conduct, reporting, and replication. This sparsity contributes to research waste and undermines the integrity of systematic reviews. Pre-2026, there is a critical need for tools that enhance protocol completeness and transparency, aligning with CONSORT and SPIRIT guidelines, while actively identifying and resolving inconsistencies in planned trial designs.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that cross-references sparse registry entries with a curated database of published protocols and methodological guidance can generate a more complete, evidence-anchored protocol draft and reliably flag discrepancies for user resolution, thereby improving methodological transparency and consistency.
proposals.method: We will develop a rule-based and NLP pipeline. First, a knowledge base will be curated from SPIRIT/ CONSORT extensions, published protocols, and methodological literature. Using entity recognition (e.g., spaCy) on a target registry entry (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov), the system will extract key elements (PICO, design). It will then query the knowledge base using semantic similarity (e.g., SBERT embeddings) to retrieve relevant evidence for inferring missing details. Discrepancy detection will employ logical rules (e.g., inconsistency between blinding and outcome type) and statistical outlier detection against the knowledge base. All inferences will be source-attributed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical tool to assist researchers in developing more complete and methodologically sound protocols directly from registry entries. It will advance the field of research-on-research by operationalizing evidence-based methodology into an auditable, semi-automated workflow, directly supporting the reproducibility and reporting ideals championed by initiatives like the EQUATOR Network.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static checklists or simple protocol templates, this system actively infers missing details from external evidence and performs adversarial discrepancy detection, creating a dynamic, substantiated draft rather than a skeleton. The integration of source attribution for methodological choices is a novel step towards auditable, evidence-based protocol development.

proposals.title: Developing a CONSORT Extension for Trial Discontinuation: A Reporting Guideline Based on Ecological Restoration Principles
proposals.motivation: Clinical trials are frequently discontinued early for non-scientific reasons (e.g., poor recruitment, loss of funding), yet no standardized, ethically sound protocol exists for this process. This leads to poor reporting, wasted resources, participant distress, and a loss of potentially valuable data for systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Drawing an analogy from the structured decommissioning protocols used in ecological restoration provides a novel framework to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying principles from ecological decommissioning (pre-assessment, phased implementation, post-monitoring) to the clinical trial lifecycle will yield a formal, consensus-based reporting guideline that improves the transparency, ethical conduct, and scientific yield of trial discontinuation processes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase guideline development project. First, a systematic scoping review will map the literature on trial discontinuation and ecological decommissioning. Second, we will convene a Delphi consensus panel of key stakeholders (trialists, ethicists, systematic reviewers, patient partners, and an ecologist) to iteratively develop checklist items. Third, we will draft the reporting guideline (a CONSORT extension) and an explanatory document, following the EQUATOR Network methodology. Finally, we will test the guideline's feasibility and clarity via a survey of trial teams who have recently discontinued a study.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formal, stakeholder-endorsed reporting guideline for the discontinuation of clinical trials, filling a critical gap in research integrity. It will provide sponsors, investigators, and journals with a concrete checklist to ensure ethical participant management, data preservation, and transparent reporting, ultimately improving the utility of discontinued trials for evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual borrowing from ecological restoration, a field with mature protocols for managing complex system transitions, to solve a persistent reporting and ethics problem in clinical research. It moves beyond describing the problem to creating a practical, consensus-based tool grounded in a transdisciplinary analogy.

proposals.title: A Living Reporting Standards (LRS) Framework: A Proof-of-Concept for Dynamic, Continuously Updated Guidelines
proposals.motivation: Static reporting guidelines (e.g., CONSORT, PRISMA) are foundational for research transparency but risk obsolescence as methodology evolves. The current model of infrequent, consensus-driven updates is slow and may not reflect emerging evidence or rapid methodological innovations. This creates a gap between best reporting practices and the actual conduct of research, potentially undermining reproducibility and the utility of published evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, living reporting standard, which continuously integrates methodological evidence and community feedback, will be more responsive to scientific advances and perceived as more useful by researchers than a static checklist.
proposals.method: We will develop a proof-of-concept Living Reporting Standards (LRS) framework for a specific guideline (e.g., PRISMA). The technical approach involves: 1) Creating a version-controlled, machine-readable repository (e.g., on GitHub) for guideline items and their supporting evidence. 2) Implementing a semi-automated pipeline to ingest and tag relevant methodological literature (e.g., via PubMed APIs) for expert review. 3) Establishing a transparent, lightweight governance model using modified Delphi processes and community voting (via GitHub Issues/Pull Requests) to propose, discuss, and ratify changes to items. 4) Conducting user testing and surveys with systematic reviewers to assess feasibility and usability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional prototype and governance model for living reporting standards, demonstrating a pathway to make guidelines more agile. It will generate empirical data on the feasibility and researcher acceptance of such a dynamic system, informing future iterations for CONSORT and other major guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While living guidelines exist for clinical care (e.g., WHO), applying this dynamic, version-controlled, and community-driven model to methodological reporting standards is novel. It moves beyond conceptual discussions of guideline updating to a testable, technical implementation.

proposals.title: Developing a machine-first reporting guideline (MFRG) framework to accelerate systematic review automation: a consensus and feasibility study
proposals.motivation: Existing reporting guidelines like CONSORT are designed for human readers, creating a bottleneck for automated systematic review (SR) tools and AI. As the volume of research accelerates, this human-centric design limits the potential for rapid, scalable evidence synthesis. A fundamental shift towards structuring research reports for primary machine consumption is needed to unlock the full potential of automation in evidence-based medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: A reporting guideline framework explicitly designed for machine-readability first will produce structured research reports that significantly improve the accuracy, speed, and completeness of data extraction by automated SR tools compared to current human-readable guidelines.
proposals.method: We will conduct a modified Delphi consensus process with experts in reporting guidelines, SR methodology, and informatics to define core data elements and a structured format (e.g., a JSON schema or XML template). This will be informed by a scoping review of existing machine-readable data standards (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov data elements, BRIDG model). We will then implement a prototype guideline and test its feasibility by comparing automated extraction performance (using rule-based NLP and pre-2026 machine learning models like BERT) on a sample of RCT reports structured with the new framework versus traditional PDFs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, consensus-based framework for machine-first reporting, directly addressing the data-sharing and interoperability challenges highlighted in recent surveys. It will provide a concrete, testable model to shift the paradigm of research reporting, laying the groundwork for more efficient, replicable, and rapid evidence synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on sharing data and code post-publication or adapting human guidelines for machines, this proposal inverts the paradigm by designing the reporting standard explicitly for automated consumption from the outset, with human-readability as a secondary output.

ideas.title: Quantifying Format-Induced Bias: Decoupling Presentation from Substance in Protocol Review
ideas.description: This research directly tests the hypothesis that format standardization introduces systematic bias in protocol evaluation, rather than neutrally improving efficiency. It employs a controlled, crossover study design where expert reviewers assess the same protocol content presented in multiple formats (highly standardized, minimally structured, and a hybrid). The primary analysis measures the divergence in scores for scientific rigor, feasibility, and clarity attributable solely to format, isolating its effect from content quality. The goal is to produce an evidence-based framework for format design that minimizes bias and supports, rather than supplants, critical assessment of scientific substance.

ideas.title: Persona-Specific, Version-Controlled Systematic Review Interfaces
ideas.description: This research develops a platform that integrates Git-like version control for every decision in a systematic review with user experience (UX) design principles tailored for specific end-user personas. The system would not just produce static, persona-specific reports, but would allow clinicians, policymakers, or patients to interactively explore the 'commit history' of the review—such as why a study was excluded or how a data extraction changed—through a interface designed for their specific needs and literacy levels. This moves transparency from a technical appendix to the core of the user experience, enabling trust and understanding by making the review's evolution personally relevant and navigable. The synergy enables a new form of living, explainable evidence synthesis where the rigor of version control directly enhances the credibility and utility of tailored reporting.

ideas.title: Adapting the CONSORT Translation and Cultural Adaptation Model for PRISMA
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to solve the problem of making the PRISMA guideline accessible for non-English speakers and different research settings by drawing an analogy from the field of Clinical Trial Reporting. The analogous problem was the need to globalize the CONSORT statement, which was solved through a formalized, multi-step translation and cultural adaptation model ensuring conceptual equivalence. The adapted idea is to apply this proven, structured model—involving forward translation, synthesis, back-translation, expert committee review, and pre-testing—specifically to PRISMA. This would create a rigorous, reproducible protocol for producing culturally and linguistically adapted versions of PRISMA that maintain the guideline's scientific integrity while being truly usable in diverse global research contexts.

ideas.title: Adaptive Protocol Synthesis and Reporting (APSR) System
ideas.description: This research proposes an Adaptive Protocol Synthesis and Reporting (APSR) system. It begins by using machine learning to infer a detailed, structured study protocol from sparse public registry entries. Crucially, the system then monitors the protocol's complexity, the review stage (e.g., ethics, results), and stakeholder needs to dynamically recommend or generate tailored reporting formats—from a streamlined checklist for a simple amendment to a comprehensive, justification-heavy document for a high-risk trial. This creates a feedback loop where the synthesized protocol informs adaptive reporting, and reporting gaps identified during review can prompt protocol refinements, ensuring coherence and reducing redundancy.

ideas.title: Apply user experience design to reporting guidelines

ideas.title: Provenance-Tracked Systematic Reviews with Structured Rationale Logging
ideas.description: This system requires reviewers to log a structured rationale for every consequential decision during a systematic review, capturing the rule applied, the evidence considered, and the subjective reasoning used. This creates a machine-readable provenance graph that explicitly links each output (a search strategy, a list of included studies, an extracted data point) to the chain of decisions and justifications that produced it. The version control system then tracks the evolution of this provenance graph and its associated rationales, not just the final documents. This enables true audit trails, allows for the analysis of decision patterns across reviews, and directly supports the reproducibility of complex, judgment-based scholarly workflows.

ideas.title: Automated, Logically-Consistent Protocol Generation and Repair from Registry Data
ideas.description: This research develops a system that first reverse-engineers a structured clinical trial protocol draft from incomplete or inconsistent registry entries (like ClinicalTrials.gov). It then applies computational logic checking—using formal methods such as model checking or theorem proving—to this generated draft. The key synergy is the system's ability to detect logical flaws (e.g., eligibility criteria that contradict the intervention, mismatched endpoints and study design) and use those findings to either automatically propose repairs to the protocol or to flag specific, problematic inconsistencies in the source registry data for human review. This creates a bidirectional tool that improves both protocol quality and the integrity of public registry information.

ideas.title: Verifiable Process Implementation for Systematic Reviews: An Evidence-Based Workflow System
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a digital workflow system for evidence synthesis that mandates the creation of verifiable process artifacts. Instead of a passive guideline, it provides an integrated platform where key conduct decisions—search strategy development, screening calibration, data extraction checks—are documented in real-time with immutable timestamps and versioning. This system enables both self-audit and external verification that the reported methods were actually followed, directly linking process to output. The goal is to shift the standard from claiming a rigorous process to demonstrably providing evidence of one.

ideas.title: Decommissioning Protocol for Clinical Trials: A Framework from Ecological Restoration
ideas.description: This idea proposes a formal protocol for the planned discontinuation of a clinical trial, drawing a direct analogy from the field of ecological restoration. Just as ecologists use detailed decommissioning plans for human-made structures to ensure a safe and monitored return to a new stable state, this protocol provides a checklist for trial sponsors and investigators. The checklist would require: 1) a pre-discontinuation assessment documenting the trial's current 'ecosystem' (participant status, data pipelines, vendor contracts), 2) a phased implementation plan to minimize disruption (orderly participant notification, data finalization, resource reallocation), and 3) a mandatory post-discontinuation report analyzing the decision's impact, the final data landscape, and lessons learned, ensuring the process is transparent, ethically sound, and contributes to the meta-science of trial conduct.

ideas.title: Evidence-Anchored Protocol Generation with Discrepancy Detection and Source Attribution
ideas.description: This system addresses the inherent sparsity of registry data by generating a trial protocol draft anchored in external evidence. It cross-references the target registry entry with a curated database of published protocols and methodological literature to infer and propose detailed design elements. Crucially, it identifies and highlights discrepancies between the registry entry and common practice or logical consistency, requiring user resolution. The final output includes source attributions for key methodological decisions, creating an auditable and substantiated protocol foundation rather than a simple skeleton.

ideas.title: Version-Controlled, AI-Augmented Single-Reviewer Workflows for Systematic Reviews
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a hybrid systematic review workflow where a single human reviewer is primarily responsible for screening and data extraction, supported by an AI assistant that flags uncertainties and suggests decisions. Crucially, every action—the initial search, the AI's suggestions, the human's overrides, and the final inclusion decisions—is committed as a version-controlled trace in a Git-like repository. This creates not just efficiency but a dynamic, living record that allows for automated checks on consistency, the identification of decision drift, and the ability to 'replay' the review with modified AI models or decision rules to test the robustness of conclusions.

ideas.title: Provenance-Aware Protocol Standardization for Transparent and Reproducible Research Workflows
ideas.description: This research investigates the development and implementation of a standardized, machine-readable format for experimental and analytical protocols. Crucially, this format is designed to integrate with data provenance frameworks, so that each step in the standardized protocol automatically logs its execution parameters, inputs, and outputs. This synergy allows the lineage of any published result to be programmatically traced back through the analysis and data collection steps to the raw data, all within the structured context of the protocol itself. The outcome is a system that simultaneously improves human usability and review efficiency through standardization while providing an automated, formal foundation for assessing reproducibility and computational rigor.

ideas.title: Agile Protocol Refinement: Iterative Development of Standardized Review Formats Based on Usability Feedback
ideas.description: This research investigates the application of agile project management principles to the development and validation of systematic review protocol formats. Instead of a one-time standardization, the protocol format itself is treated as a minimum viable product (MVP) and refined through iterative sprints, where specific format elements (e.g., data visualization templates, decision log structures) are A/B tested within active review teams. Continuous feedback from reviewers and stakeholders on usability and efficiency is quantitatively and qualitatively gathered at the end of each sprint to guide the next iteration of format improvements. This creates an evidence-based, living standardization process that optimizes the protocol as a tool for review conduct, dynamically balancing content rigor with user-centric design.

ideas.title: Standardizing Protocol Structure as a 'Grammar' to Improve Scientific Communication
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that protocol quality and usability are driven primarily by their descriptive content. Drawing an analogy from bioinformatics, where the imposition of strict, standardized file formats (e.g., FASTQ, BED) revolutionized data sharing and computational analysis by separating content from presentation and enabling automated validation, this idea proposes a similar solution for research protocols. The adapted research would involve designing a formal, machine-readable schema or 'grammar' for protocols that defines mandatory and optional structural elements (e.g., structured hypotheses, stepwise methods with parameter fields, predefined analysis pipelines). The study would then empirically investigate whether this enforced format standardization improves usability for researchers and efficiency for reviewers more than traditional, content-focused guidelines alone, by reducing ambiguity, enabling automated completeness checks, and facilitating direct comparison and replication.

ideas.title: SPIRIT-AIR: An Aviation-Style Safety Assurance Framework for Adaptive Platform and Mega-Trials
ideas.description: This research proposes SPIRIT-AIR, a novel framework that integrates the structured protocol reporting of SPIRIT with the rigorous, hierarchical safety assurance and verification methodologies from aviation (e.g., failure mode and effects analysis, mandatory operational checklists, and crew resource management principles). It is specifically designed for the dynamic environment of adaptive platform trials and international mega-trials. The system introduces a live, digital 'protocol dashboard' with pre-flight, in-flight, and post-flight checkpoints that continuously monitor trial integrity, intervention safety, and data quality across multiple sites and arms. This enables trial steering committees and regulators to manage complex, evolving trial protocols with the same level of proven, real-time operational safety assurance used in aviation, fundamentally improving the reliability and trustworthiness of large-scale adaptive research.

ideas.title: Context-Adaptive Evidence Reporting System (CAERS)
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a dynamic reporting system that moves beyond static persona-based templates. The system uses a modular evidence backbone and a context engine that assesses the user's immediate task (e.g., diagnosis, resource planning, personal understanding), urgency, and operational environment to dynamically assemble a tailored report view. It validates this approach by measuring reductions in time-to-insight and error rates across simulated, high-stakes decision scenarios compared to traditional, fixed-format reports.

ideas.title: Semantic Protocol Version Control with Impact-Aware Governance
ideas.description: This system combines Git-like versioning with a formal ontology for clinical trial protocols, treating protocol elements (endpoints, interventions, criteria) as versioned objects, not just text. It automatically classifies changes by their semantic impact (e.g., 'primary endpoint modified' vs. 'typographical correction') and routes them through appropriate governance and approval pathways. The result is a traceable audit trail that links every significant protocol change directly to its scientific justification and regulatory approval status, ensuring critical modifications are never obscured by minor textual edits.

ideas.title: Adapt Modular Engineering Standards to Create SPIRIT Extensions for Complex Trial Designs
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of creating SPIRIT extensions for specific trial types (e.g., pragmatic, cluster-randomized) by drawing an analogy from civil engineering's approach to building codes. In that field, international codes (like the IBC) serve as a foundation, with specialized, modular annexes created for distinct contexts like seismic zones or essential facilities. The analogous solution is to develop tailored, context-specific standards that plug into a core framework. Adapted to clinical trial reporting, the research would involve defining a minimal core SPIRIT checklist for all interventional trials, then developing rigorously validated, standalone extension modules for complex designs. Each module (e.g., for pragmatic trials) would specify only the new or adapted checklist items pertinent to that design's unique reporting needs—such as eligibility criteria flexibility or cluster-level interventions—allowing trialists to combine the core with relevant modules without being burdened by irrelevant items from other designs.

ideas.description: This research develops a system that uses trial registry entries to auto-generate a full study protocol. Crucially, it goes beyond simple population by analyzing the generated protocol's structure and usability metrics to identify which standardized format elements (e.g., specific section ordering, data presentation templates) most improve reviewer efficiency and clarity. The system then applies these insights to refine its own generation templates, creating a feedback loop where registry data continuously informs and optimizes protocol format standards, thereby enhancing the quality and consistency of future automated protocol generation.

ideas.title: Inverse Protocol Generation via Paleontological Reconstruction Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from paleontological reconstruction to solve the inverse problem of generating a clinical trial protocol from registry data. In paleontology, scientists infer the complete form and function of an extinct organism from fossilized bones by applying anatomical constraints and models from related species. Analogously, the proposed system will treat a trial registry entry (e.g., from ClinicalTrials.gov) as a sparse 'skeleton' of key design elements (PICO, endpoints, design type). It will then auto-populate a detailed protocol skeleton by applying constraints from a knowledge base of protocol standards (SPIRIT-CONSORT), statistical design principles, and templates from similar past trials, thereby inferring plausible, structured content for methodology, procedures, and analysis plans that are absent from the registry.

ideas.title: Apply agile project management to scoping reviews

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Version Control to Systematic Reviews
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from evolutionary biology, where phylogenetic trees track divergent lineages from a common ancestor, to the problem of tracking changes in systematic reviews. In phylogenetics, tools model branching evolutionary histories from shared data. Applying this analogically, we propose a 'Review Phylogeny' system. Instead of a linear Git-like history, each major methodological decision (e.g., altering a PICO element, applying a new exclusion criterion) creates a branch from the prior review state. This allows multiple, parallel 'lineages' of the review (e.g., different team interpretations, alternative analytic choices) to be developed, visually compared, and potentially merged. The solution provides a structured, branching audit trail that explicitly maps how different decisions lead to divergent evidence syntheses, enhancing reproducibility and the study of methodological sensitivity.

ideas.title: Apply software testing frameworks to research reporting

ideas.title: Adapting Software Version Control to Create Versioned, Forkable Living Reviews
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static publication model by drawing a direct analogy from software development's solution to managing evolving projects. In open-source software, distributed version control systems like Git solve the problem of collaborative, continuous development by tracking all changes, allowing for branching and forking, and maintaining a complete history. This solution is adapted to the research context of living systematic reviews. The adapted idea is to implement a version control framework for reviews, where the evidence synthesis is treated as a dynamic codebase. A canonical 'main' branch is updated continuously with new evidence, while the community can create forks to explore methodological alternatives. A formal process akin to pull requests facilitates peer-reviewed integration of updates, and semantic versioning tags (e.g., a Major version for a reversal of a key finding, a Patch for corrected typos) provide immediate clarity on the nature and importance of changes. This creates a living, collaborative, and fully auditable review system with dynamic reporting standards built into its versioning and contribution model.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Extract protocol from messy records
ideas.description: Develop methods to reconstruct a TIDieR-compliant description from fragmented clinical notes and audit trails.

ideas.title: Causal Inference Study on SPIRIT's Impact on Protocol Quality Using Matched Controls
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference framework to isolate the effect of SPIRIT adoption on protocol completeness and transparency. It constructs a longitudinal dataset of protocol artifacts, pairing SPIRIT-adopting projects with statistically matched non-adopting projects based on pre-adoption characteristics and trends. The analysis uses a difference-in-differences model to compare the change in quality metrics (e.g., checklist fulfillment, disclosure clarity) between the two groups after the treatment group's adoption. This design robustly addresses selection bias to determine if SPIRIT itself drives improvement, rather than merely being associated with higher-quality projects.

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Urban Planning's 'Design Charrettes' for Patient-Centered Protocol Co-Creation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from participatory urban planning—the design charrette—to the challenge of involving patients in clinical trial protocol design. In urban planning, design charrettes are intensive, collaborative workshops that bring diverse stakeholders together to rapidly co-create and refine master plans through iterative modeling and feedback. The adapted research idea proposes 'Protocol Charrettes': structured, time-limited workshops where patient partners, clinicians, and trial designers intensively collaborate to co-create specific trial protocol sections. By employing rapid prototyping of protocol language and integrating real-world feasibility constraints in real-time, this method aims to move beyond advisory input to genuine co-creation, producing more pragmatic and patient-informed protocol drafts for complex trial elements like eligibility, burden, and endpoints.

ideas.title: Challenge that all items are equally essential

ideas.title: Adapting Nuclear Power Plant Procedural Adherence Verification to Clinical Trial Protocol Execution
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of transferring aviation safety checklists to clinical trials by drawing an analogy from nuclear power plant operations, another ultra-high-reliability field. The analogous problem is ensuring absolute adherence to complex, safety-critical written procedures under operational pressure. The nuclear industry solves this with layered verification: pre-job briefings, real-time independent verification by a second operator (IVO) for critical steps, and mandatory sign-offs. Adapted to clinical trial protocols, this creates a formal 'Protocol Use and Adherence' (PUA) framework. For defined critical trial procedures (e.g., investigational product administration, randomization, specific sample collections), the research team must conduct a pre-procedure briefing, execute the steps with real-time independent verification by a second qualified staff member, and obtain a dual sign-off in an electronic trial log before proceeding. This system aims to drastically reduce protocol deviations and execution errors, enhancing trial data integrity and participant safety.

ideas.title: The Aviation-Style Continuous Verification Protocol (CVP)
ideas.description: This idea synthesizes the living document concept with aviation's rigorous document control to create a Continuous Verification Protocol (CVP). The CVP is structured like an aircraft maintenance manual, with a core, immutable master protocol and appendices for procedures, checklists, and site-specific instructions that are version-controlled and updated in real-time based on operational data and safety reports. Each update triggers an aviation-style verification loop—a mandatory, documented check by independent roles (e.g., medical monitor, data manager, statistician) against a pre-defined hazard matrix before implementation. This transforms the protocol from a static reference into an active risk-management system, providing an auditable trail of all adaptations while maintaining the stringent change control required for regulatory acceptance in clinical research.

ideas.title: Adversarial AI Review for Investigator-Led Protocol Refinement
ideas.description: This research tests a system where the investigator retains primary authorship of the initial protocol draft, preserving their expert insight. The core innovation is an adversarial AI framework that subjects this human-generated draft to intensive, multi-perspective simulated review. The AI modules act as devil's advocates, challenging assumptions, identifying methodological biases, uncovering feasibility pitfalls, and flagging regulatory inconsistencies. This process creates a rigorous, iterative feedback loop that strengthens the investigator's original design rather than replacing it, leading to more robust, defensible, and executable protocols.

ideas.title: Establish a Living, Community-Driven PRISMA Framework with Modular Extensions
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of keeping the PRISMA checklist current with methodological advances by drawing an analogy from software engineering's web standards development. The analogous field uses a 'living standards' model with a stable core and modular, community-driven extensions. The adapted research idea is to establish a living, community-driven PRISMA framework. This would involve maintaining a core PRISMA checklist while creating a formal governance structure for specialist working groups to develop, test, and endorse modular 'PRISMA Extensions' for distinct new methods. This system would allow the guideline ecosystem to evolve rapidly and responsively without constant, disruptive overhauls of the central document.

ideas.title: Single-Reviewer Validation via AI-Enabled Statistical Process Control
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that two independent human reviewers are necessary for robust peer review by drawing an analogy from industrial quality control. In manufacturing, Statistical Process Control (SPC) successfully shifted from exhaustive manual inspection to a system where automated sensors monitor processes and a single engineer interprets the data. Adapted to research, an AI system would be trained to perform continuous, automated checks on submitted manuscripts for objective, reviewable criteria (e.g., statistical methods, ethical compliance, data availability statements). The single human reviewer's role evolves to synthesizing these AI-generated diagnostics with their own expert evaluation of the manuscript's novelty, significance, and reasoning, thereby creating a more efficient, augmented, and potentially more consistent validation process.

ideas.title: Dual problem: automate extraction of PRISMA data

ideas.title: Modular, Logic-Driven Protocol Checklists with Deterministic Adaptation
ideas.description: This system replaces a fully dynamic guideline with a suite of pre-defined, static checklist modules for different trial aspects (e.g., screening, consent, monitoring). A rule engine, using pre-specified inputs like protocol risk classification and participant criteria, automatically selects and assembles the appropriate modules for a given trial or participant cohort. This ensures adaptation is not arbitrary but follows a transparent, pre-approved algorithm, maintaining auditability and regulatory compliance. The result is a consistent, complexity-responsive tool that preserves the reliability of standardization while eliminating unnecessary steps for lower-risk scenarios.

ideas.title: SPIRIT-AI: An adaptive, single-reviewer platform with embedded AI validation for trial protocol development
ideas.description: This project develops an integrated software platform that combines interactive SPIRIT checklist templates with embedded AI validation agents. The platform is designed to support a single human reviewer by providing real-time, context-sensitive AI checks on protocol drafts against SPIRIT criteria, flagging potential omissions or inconsistencies. Crucially, the system is adaptive; it uses the reviewer's interactions and overrides to learn domain-specific nuances and improve its suggestions, thereby personalizing the SPIRIT guidance. This creates a synergistic loop where the tool not only enforces reporting standards but also evolves to become a more intelligent co-pilot, making rigorous, SPIRIT-compliant protocol development more efficient and accessible for teams with limited resources.

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Versioning to Trial Protocol Lineages
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from phylogenetics—the reconstruction of evolutionary trees from shared derived traits—to the problem of tracking complex protocol development. In trial protocol development, multiple branches (e.g., for different regions, sub-studies, or pilot tests) often emerge from a core protocol. The adapted system would treat each protocol version or amendment as a 'taxon' and use key, deliberate modifications as shared derived characters. Algorithms would then infer the most parsimonious branching history, creating a visual and analytical 'phylogenetic tree' of the protocol. This provides an automatic, evidence-based map of the protocol's evolution, clarifying the provenance of each branch and the specific changes that define each lineage, far beyond simple linear version history.

ideas.title: Apply Model Checking from Hardware Verification to Clinical Trial Protocols
ideas.description: This idea adapts the formal method of model checking, a cornerstone of hardware verification in electrical and computer engineering, to the domain of clinical trial protocol design. In hardware, model checking tools exhaustively explore a circuit's state space to verify it adheres to logical and temporal specifications, catching design flaws early. The adapted research idea involves creating a formal model of a clinical trial protocol—encoding its steps, decision branches, and patient pathways as a finite-state system—and then using a model checker to verify it against formalized rules for safety, logical flow, and regulatory requirements. This would automatically detect internal inconsistencies, such as contradictory eligibility criteria, unreachable study endpoints, or violations of necessary temporal sequences in patient care, thereby improving protocol robustness and patient safety before trial deployment.

ideas.title: Adapting the Chain of Custody from Forensic Science for Research Data Provenance
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'chain of custody' framework from forensic science to solve the problem of documenting data lineage in computational research. In forensics, a meticulous, chronological log is maintained for every piece of physical evidence to prove its integrity from collection to courtroom. Adapting this solution, we propose a research data provenance system that formally documents the complete lineage of each scientific conclusion back to the original study data. This system would mandate logging every entity (person or software) that accesses or transforms the data, the timestamp, and the nature of the operation, creating an immutable, auditable trail. This forensic-inspired protocol would provide a robust, legally-sound method to ensure data integrity, prevent undisclosed manipulation, and allow independent verification of the entire research workflow.

ideas.title: Protocol Drafting via a Foldit-Style Gamified Crowdsourcing Platform
ideas.description: This idea challenges the investigator-led protocol drafting assumption by drawing an analogy from computational biochemistry and citizen science. The analogous field used the 'Foldit' game to solve the complex problem of protein folding by crowdsourcing it as a gamified puzzle, yielding solutions that algorithms and experts alone missed. The adapted research idea is to create a similar gamified, crowdsourced platform for generating initial clinical or preclinical research protocol drafts. By translating protocol components (like population definition, intervention details, comparator selection, and outcome measures) into interactive, game-like modules with rulesets and collaborative/competitive incentives, the platform would harness collective human intelligence to produce a variety of novel, structured protocol drafts. This tests whether such a method can efficiently generate scientifically sound and creatively diverse starting points that challenge conventional, single-investigator drafting approaches.

ideas.title: Relax the one-size-fits-all checklist assumption
ideas.description: Design modular checklists where items are selected based on review type and complexity.

ideas.title: SPIRIT-CAD: A Domain-Specific Language and IDE for Protocol Specification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Electronic Design Automation (EDA), where Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) and specialized IDEs are used to manage complex chip specifications. The analogous solution uses languages like VHDL within IDEs that offer syntax checking, component libraries, and automated validation against design rule checklists to ensure standards compliance before physical fabrication. Adapted to the clinical research context, we propose developing SPIRIT-Lang, a Domain-Specific Language for formally specifying trial protocols, and SPIRIT-CAD, an Integrated Development Environment for it. The IDE would provide intelligent, context-aware templates mapped to SPIRIT checklist items, real-time validation and feedback, and automated export to standardized protocol documents (PDF, Word). This transforms the SPIRIT checklist from a passive, post-hoc verification tool into an active framework that guides and enforces rigorous protocol design from the outset, significantly improving protocol quality and regulatory compliance.

ideas.title: Executable Protocol with Immutable Decision Logs and Gated Amendments
ideas.description: This system treats the protocol as a version-controlled executable document, where the core design is locked but can evolve through pre-specified amendment pathways. All potential modifications are defined as conditional logic before trial initiation. During the trial, any proposed change triggers an immutable decision log entry, requiring justification and approval before the relevant data is available for analysis. This maintains rigorous scientific and regulatory integrity by ensuring all adaptations are traceable, deliberate, and distinct from post-hoc data exploration, transforming the living document into a disciplined, auditable process.

ideas.title: Adaptive Clinical Trial Protocols via Dynamic Risk-Based Control
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that clinical trial quality oversight requires a fixed, one-size-fits-all monitoring checklist. Drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's adaptive flight control systems, which modify control laws based on real-time flight conditions, we propose a framework for adaptive, dynamic protocol guidelines. The analogous solution involves systems that use continuous sensor input to adjust performance parameters. Adapted to the clinical trial context, the research would develop a protocol engine that integrates a live 'trial state vector'—comprising metrics for complexity, risk, and performance—to dynamically govern the application of a modular set of monitoring and quality control activities. This moves oversight from a static checklist to a responsive, state-aware system that allocates verification resources precisely where and when they are needed based on the trial's current operational landscape.

ideas.title: Apply computational reproducibility frameworks from computer science

ideas.title: Single-Reviewer Process Augmented by an Adversarial AI Simulating Missing Reviewer Perspectives
ideas.description: This research tests a system where a single human reviewer is supported not by a compliance-checking AI, but by an AI explicitly configured to act as an adversarial proxy for absent reviewers. The AI generates detailed, stance-specific critical reviews (e.g., challenging methodological assumptions, questioning novelty, or proposing alternative interpretations) based on the submitted manuscript. The human reviewer must formally engage with these AI-generated critiques, documenting their response and reasoning for acceptance or rejection. This design maintains human oversight and deliberation while systematically injecting the cognitive diversity and challenge traditionally provided by a multi-reviewer panel, directly mitigating the risks of automation bias and intellectual stagnation.

ideas.title: The SPIRIT Embedded Tutor and Protocol Diagnostic System
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static templates by embedding an intelligent, interactive tutor within the protocol development environment. It uses a library of annotated case studies and common failure scenarios to teach SPIRIT principles through active problem-solving. As a researcher drafts a protocol, the system's diagnostic engine performs a coherence check, flagging inconsistencies between sections (e.g., between objectives and analysis plans), identifying vague or ambiguous text, and suggesting evidence-based improvements. The goal is to build methodological competence and critical thinking, ensuring the final protocol is not just checklist-compliant but scientifically robust and logically coherent.

ideas.title: Adapting Modular Satellite Architecture for SPIRIT in Platform Trials
ideas.description: This idea extends the SPIRIT guideline for complex platform trials by drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's solution to scaling satellite constellations. The analogous solution uses a modular architecture where a core satellite 'bus' manages essential functions and standard interfaces for swappable mission 'payloads'. Adapted to clinical trials, the research proposes a new SPIRIT module that defines a core protocol 'bus' for the stable elements of a platform trial (master protocol, shared infrastructure, common control) and standardizes the interface specifications for dynamically adding, modifying, or removing intervention 'modules'. This would enable the scalable, parallel, and efficient design of international mega-trials and complex platform trials by decoupling the long-term platform management from the lifecycle of individual interventions.

ideas.title: Bridging the Semantic Gap: Integrated NLP and Formal Verification for Protocol Consistency
ideas.description: This system addresses the abstraction gap by first using NLP techniques to parse natural language protocol documents, identifying actions, conditions, and temporal orders to construct an initial formal representation. It then applies computational logic checking to this extracted model for internal consistency. Crucially, it compares this NLP-derived model against any hand-crafted formal specification, flagging semantic discrepancies for human review. This integrated approach ensures the verified model is grounded in the actual protocol text, not just an idealized abstraction.

ideas.title: Cryptographically-Verifiable Data Provenance with Automated Integrity Validation
ideas.description: This system creates a trustworthy lineage for scientific conclusions by anchoring the entire data provenance chain in cryptographically-secure, tamper-evident records. It mandates the initial registration of study protocols, raw data, and analysis code, generating immutable hashes that serve as trust anchors for all subsequent processing steps. Automated validators continuously check the consistency of each documented transformation against the actual data outputs, flagging any integrity violations. This approach ensures the provenance record itself is auditable and resistant to retroactive manipulation, addressing the core flaw of trusting undocumented or mutable source inputs.
